segments.s240-g385: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The phrase has the order: conjunction + demonstrative + noun + adverb + verb. این داستان 'this tale' is the direct object, سربسر is an adverb meaning 'from head to tail / entirely' placed before the verb for emphasis, و بشنوی is 2nd singular present/subjunctive (poetic/future sense) 'you hear / you will hear'. Together: 'When you hear this whole tale...'.
segments.s240-g386: This segment is a prepositional/nominal clause followed by the verb شوی (2nd sg. present/subjunctive 'you become'). از اکوان literally 'from the akvān' (poetic plural for the heavens/worlds) begins the phrase; سوی is a directional preposition 'toward / in the direction of'; کین بیژن is a genitive construction without the ezāfe (poetic word order) meaning 'the hatred/vengeance of Bijan'; شوی is the verb 'you become' linking back to the temporal clause. Word order is poetic: prepositional phrase + directional phrase + possessive noun phrase + verb.
segments.s240-g387: A single punctuation segment (period) marking a pause before the next imperative clause; no lexical items to analyze here.
segments.s240-g388: یکی آرزو کن is an imperative instruction: یکی 'one/a single' modifies آرزو 'wish' and کن is the 2nd-person-singular imperative 'do/make' (i.e., 'make one wish'). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'). تا here functions as a subordinating particle ('so that / in order that') leading into the purpose/result clause; از + هوا is a prepositional phrase 'from (the) hava', where هوا in poetic context commonly means 'desire / whim / mood' (the rest of that clause continues in the following line).
segments.s240-g1: تو is the emphatic 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. بر + noun here is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/unto' (بر = to/toward) and introduces the addressee 'کردگار' (the Creator). روان و خرد are two coordinated nouns ('soul and wisdom') that function as instruments/aspects connected to the upcoming verb phrase — they belong syntactically to the next clause rather than forming a complete predicate here.
segments.s240-g2: ستایش گزین is an imperative/jussive sequence: گزین (choose/select) commands offering 'praise' (ستایش). تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that') and is followed by an interrogative/indefinite چه ('what') + اندر خورد, with خورد (here 'to befall/occur') forming the verb of the subordinate clause; the verbal idea comes at the end in typical older Persian word order.
segments.s240-g3: ببین is the 2nd‑person imperative 'look/see'. ای is the vocative marker 'O', which directly addresses the following epithet. خردمند روشن‌روان is a compound epithet ('wise, bright‑souled'); adjectives/nouns are placed after the vocative to describe the addressee.
segments.s240-g4: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/—/how'). چون here means 'how' (in what manner). باید is a modal 'must/ought' and combines with the following infinitival construction او را ستودن توان, where او را is the definite object ('him') marked by را, ستودن is the infinitive 'to praise', and توان is the auxiliary 'can/able to' placed after the infinitive — together yielding 'how one must/can praise him'.
segments.s240-g5: همه دانش ما is the subject phrase 'all our knowledge/learning' (همه = all, دانش = knowledge, ما = our). به + predicate (بیچارگیست = بیچارگی است) links the subject to a predicated state: 'is in/to misery' — here به has a sense of 'into/toward' making the copular sense 'is [one of] misery'.
segments.s240-g6: به بیچارگان marks the beneficiary/target 'for the wretched'. بر before the verb ('بر ... گریست') means 'over/for' (weep over). بباید is an impersonal/modal 'it is necessary/one must' and is followed by the verb گریست ('to weep') placed at the end in archaic word order, yielding 'one must weep for the wretched.'
segments.s240-g7: تو خستو شو is an imperative: خستو (tired/weary) with the imperative verb شو 'become' = 'become weary' or idiomatically 'be content/cease striving'. آنرا is the demonstrative object 'that (thing)', and the relative clause که هست و یکیست ('which exists and is one') follows, with هست and یکیست serving as copulas describing آنرا.
segments.s240-g8: روان و خرد را uses را to topicalize/mark 'soul and wisdom' ('as for soul and mind'). جزین is a fused/poetic form of 'جز این' meaning 'except this'; it modifies راه ('way/method'). The negation نیست at the end yields the formulaic conclusion 'there is no way for soul and wisdom except this', with typical SOV/verb‑final order.
segments.s240-g97: همانگه is an adverbial temporal phrase 'at that moment'. پدید آمد is a compound verb (پدید + آمد) meaning 'appeared/appeared into view' with آمد in the simple past. از دشت is a locative phrase 'from the plain' (preposition + noun). باز follows and functions adverbially 'again/re‑emerged', modifying پدید آمد; word order is typical poetic Persian where temporal phrase + verb + locative + adverb appear in that order.
segments.s240-g98: سپهبد is the subject (the spahbed). برانگیخت is a past transitive verb 'roused/raised' (simple past). آن تند تاز is the direct object: آن 'that/the' + two attributive adjectives/compound nouns تند (swift) و تاز (fierce/impetuous) forming a compound epithet 'that swift, fierce charger'. The clause follows a subject–verb–object order typical of older Persian.
segments.s240-g99: کمان را is the definite direct object marked by را ('the bow'). بزه کرد is a past verbal phrase where کرد is the light verb 'did/made' attached to بزه (here functioning as the action on the bow — in context 'made/drew/bent the bow'); و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. از باد اسپ is a prepositional phrase 'from/with the wind of the steed' (از = from, باد = 'wind', اسپ = 'horse/steed') giving the source/manner for the following action.
segments.s240-g100: بینداخت is a past transitive verb 'threw/sent (forth)'. تیری is the definite/indefinite object 'an arrow' (here object of بینداخت). چو is the comparative/simile marker 'like/as'. آذر گشسپ forms a name/epithet: آذر here functions as part of the proper name or epithet and گشسپ is the hero 'Goshasp', so the simile compares the arrow to that of (or like) Azar‑Goshasp.
segments.s240-g101: همان کو is an archaic relative phrase 'the very one who' (همان = 'that/same', کو here functions as the relative 'who/that' in older Persian). کمان کیان is a possessive/genitive construction 'the bow of the Kians/the royal bow' (noun + possessor), and درکشید is a past transitive verb 'drew/pulled' (here 'drew' the bow). Word order places the relative + possessed noun before the verb, as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s240-g102: دگر باره is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'again/once more' (دگر 'again' + باره 'time/instance'). شد is the past of شدن 'became/happened'. گور ازو ناپدید is a clause with گور 'the grave' as subject, ازو 'from him' (prepositional/possessive sense), and ناپدید 'vanished/was unseen' as predicate — literally 'the grave from him became unseen', i.e. 'the grave vanished for him'.
segments.s240-g103: همی is a durative/adverbial particle 'kept/continually' (here 'kept on'). تاخت is the past verb 'galloped/rushed'. اسپ is the subject 'steed(s)'. اندِران ('اندران') is a locative/prepositional adverb 'among them/therein' and پهن دشت is a noun phrase 'the wide/broad plain' (adjective + noun) indicating where the action takes place. The sequence gives continuous motion across a wide plain.
segments.s240-g104: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'. سه روز و سه شب is the time span 'three days and three nights'. برو بر گذشت is an older/periphrastic verbal expression meaning 'went by/passed' (برو here has a directional/intensifying sense and بر + گذشت convey 'passed over/by'); together they form 'three days and three nights passed.'
segments.s240-g65: چنین گفت رستم که is a reporting clause: چنین 'thus' (fronted adverb) + گفت (past 'said') + رستم (proper name). که introduces the content clause ('that' / direct speech). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'Thus Rostam said that...'.
segments.s240-g66: نترسد is the third-person-present/future negative ('does not/will not fear'). پرستنده تخت تو is the subject noun phrase: پرستنده 'worshipper/devotee' + تخت 'throne' (possessed) + تو 'your' (poetic order places تو after تخت). Together: 'the worshipper of your throne will not fear.'
segments.s240-g67: نه ... و نه ... و نه ... is the classical Persian correlative negative 'neither ... nor ... nor ...'; the list items follow in sequence: دیو 'demon', شیر 'lion', نر اژدها 'male dragon' (نر modifies the noun that follows).
segments.s240-g68: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. شمشیر تیزم combines شمشیر 'sword' + تیز 'sharp' with the enclitic -م (my), so 'my sharp sword.' نیابد رها is a compound predication: نیابد ('does not/ will not find' — third-person) + رها ('release/free[dom]') = 'will not find release' i.e. 'will not be freed.'
segments.s240-g69: برون شد is a simple past/perfect 'came out / sprang forth.' بنخچیر is the noun 'prey / booty' (the thing that appears). چون introduces a simile 'like/as' and نره شیر is 'male lion' (نره modifying شیر). The phrase describes the prey appearing like a male lion.
segments.s240-g70: کمندی 'a snare/lasso' is the subject of the second clause. بدست here functions as 'into the hand of' (in combination with the following genitive), so بدست اژدهایی = 'into a dragon's hand.' بزیر 'down' indicates motion downward. The clause means 'a snare fell down into a dragon's hand.'
segments.s240-g71: به دشتی is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/at a plain' (به + دشتی). کجا 'where' introduces the relative/adverbial clause. داشت is past 'had/kept' and چوپان گله 'a shepherd (subject) [had] a flock' (گله = flock following the head چوپان in poetic order).
segments.s240-g72: وزانسو (poetic compound equivalent to 'وز آن سو' = 'from that side / on that side') is an adverbial phrase. گذر داشت 'had a passage' = 'there was a pass/track' (داشت used existentially). گور یله is a noun + adjective: گور 'onager/wild ass' modified by یله 'wild / unrestrained', i.e. 'a wild onager' roaming the pass.
segments.s240-g17: به یک دم زدن is an adverbial phrase meaning “by/with a single breath/instant (or a single blow)”; here زدن is used nominally/idiomatically as the act of striking (literally “a striking”). رستی is the 2nd-person singular archaic/past-present form of رستن meaning “you are freed/saved/escape” (classical Persian often uses this form to mean a present/simple action). از جان و تن is a simple from+two-noun complement “from soul and body” specifying what you are freed from.
segments.s240-g18: همی here is an emphatic/archaic particle (“indeed/so”); بس functions as an intensifier/limiter “enough/merely/that alone.” The verbless clause بزرگ آیدت خویشتن uses بزرگ (adjective “great”) with the verb آید (comes) plus clitic ت (to you), producing the idiom “it comes to you great” = “you will consider/think yourself great”; خویشتن is the emphatic object “yourself.” Word order is adjective + verb + clitic + emphatic object, typical of classical Persian stylistic inversion.
segments.s240-g19: همی again gives a continuous or habitual nuance (“keeps/continually”); بگذرد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of گذشتن used here in the sense “pass (by/over).” بر تو marks the experiencer “upon you,” and ایام تو (“your days/times”) is the subject/theme whose passing is asserted. The verb appears before its logical subject for poetic inversion.
segments.s240-g20: سرای is the noun “dwelling/house/abode” (here meaning the place of rest, i.e. the grave); جز این is a restricting phrase “except/other than this.” باشد is the subjunctive/present form of بودن (“is/there is”); آرام تو is a nominative/possessive phrase “your rest/peace/comfort.” The whole clause says that there is no other abode except this in which you are at rest — a poetic way of saying there is no other lasting home than this (death/your grave).
segments.s240-g21: نخست is an adverb “first.” از marks the object/source “of/from,” here linking to جهان‌آفرین “the world‑maker/Creator.” یاد کن is an imperative: یاد (remembrance) + کن (do) = “remember” or “make remembrance.” Word order places the adverb and source before the imperative for emphasis.
segments.s240-g22: پرستش is the noun “worship.” بر این یاد is a prepositional phrase “upon this remembrance.” بنیاد کن is an imperative construction: بنیاد (foundation) + کن (do/make) = “establish a foundation.” The command thus instructs to base worship on that remembrance; Persian places the noun phrase (object/goal) before the verbal imperative in this poetic construction.
segments.s240-g23: کزویست is a contracted classical form meaning “for/from him it is” (کز = که از, وی = او + است). گردون گردان is a poetic compound “the revolving/skirling heaven” (گردون = sky/fate, گردان = revolving). بپای is a verbal/adjectival phrase meaning “stands firm/abides” (بـ + پای = “on foot/firm” → “remains/stands”). The line asserts that the turning heavens owe their being and steadiness to Him.
segments.s240-g24: هم is an emphatic particle “also/indeed.” اویست here is a contracted literary fusion of او + است (“he is”); بر نیک و بد is a prepositional phrase “with/regarding good and bad” or “over good and evil,” and رهنمای is a nominal predicate “guide.” Together: “He too is the guide regarding good and evil.” Note: اویست is an older fused form (not the proper name Owais) and functions as the copula + pronoun.
segments.s240-g241: همی is a habitual/continuative particle here (marks repeated or ongoing action). بگذراند is 3rd-person singular past/subjunctive-tinged form meaning 'cause to pass' or 'entrust/put (something) into someone's care'; به یک تن is the indirect object 'to one person'; گله is the direct object 'flock/herd'. Word order is Subject (implied) — همی بگذراند — then the prepositional phrase marking the recipient (به یک تن) and the thing entrusted (گله).
segments.s240-g242: نشاید is an impersonal present/past-modal formula meaning 'it is not right/fit' (literally 'it should not be'); چنین 'such' modifies the following noun phrase; کار کردن is the verbal noun + infinitive construction 'to do a deed' where کردن is the light verb; یله is an adjective/adverb meaning 'loose, lax, unrestrained' modifying کردن (i.e. 'to act laxly'). The whole clause is evaluative: 'Such lax doing is not fitting.'
segments.s240-g243: سپهدار is the head noun 'the commander'. با introduces accompaniment or possession 'with'; چار here is the numeral 'four' modifying پیل 'elephants'; و links سپاه 'army' as an additional accompaniment. This is a pre-verbal noun phrase listing the commander and his forces.
segments.s240-g244: پس is a temporal/sequence adverb 'then/after that'. رستم here is the goal/target (to/at Rostam). اندر + راه is the idiom 'to take the road/way toward' (اندر used poetically for 'into/toward'); گرفتند is past plural 'they took/seized' agreeing with the compound subject implied by the preceding phrase (the commander with his elephants and army). The clause means 'they set out / took the road toward Rostam.'
segments.s240-g245: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گشتند is a past tense verb 'they turned / they came' (here 'they approached'); نزدیک + رستم is an adverbial phrase 'near Rostam'. کمان begins the next action (object carried over into the following verb clause). The clause sets the scene: 'When they drew near Rostam…'.
segments.s240-g246: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بازو 'arm' is the source from which something is drawn. برون 'out' is an adverb of motion. کرد is the simple past 'he did/pulled' and takes the preceding noun (کمان) as its object — so کمان ز بازو برون کرد = 'he drew the bow out from his arm'. و 'and' links the next verb آمد 'he came' (simple past). دمان is a noun meaning 'hem / edge' and here indicates the place or manner he approached (a stock poetic phrase meaning 'he came forward at the hem').
segments.s240-g247: بریشان is a prepositional/particle form meaning 'upon them' (بر + ایشان), marking the target of the falling missiles. ببارید is the past intransitive 'it rained / fell' (used metaphorically for missiles falling). چو ژاله میغ is a simile 'like dew (ژاله), (fell) blades (میغ)' — the image is that blades fell like dew. The verb governs the whole simile construction.
segments.s240-g248: The repeated چه ... و چه ... is an exclamatory listing 'what ... and what ...' here enumerating kinds of missiles. تیر از کمان = 'arrows from a bow'; پولاد تیغ = 'steel sword' (پولاد modifying تیغ). There is no finite verb in this fragment — it is a nominal exclamation amplifying the previous clause ('blades rained... — what arrows and what steel swords!').
segments.s240-g233: ز ترکان برآمد یکی گفت و گوی is a compact nominative phrase: a postpositional genitive 'ز ترکان' (from the Turks) followed by the verb 'برآمد' (arose/appeared). 'یکی' functions as an indefinite 'one / a certain (person)' and 'گفت و گوی' (lit. 'speech/conversation') here names him as a speaker or spokesman. Word order (source → verb → indefinite → role) is typical of epic Persian, where the relative explanation follows.
segments.s240-g234: که تنها بجنگ آمد این کینه‌جوی is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by 'که' (= 'who/that'). 'تنها' = 'alone' modifies the infinitival phrase 'بجنگ آمد' (came to fight — literal sequence: 'to-fight came'), a classical Persian periphrasis meaning 'came for the purpose of fighting.' 'این کینه‌جوی' is a nominative apposition identifying that person as 'this revenge-seeker.'
segments.s240-g235: بباید کشیدن یکایک سلیح uses the impersonal/modal 'بباید' (must/ought) followed by the verbal noun 'کشیدن' (to draw/take up), so the sense is 'it is necessary to draw.' 'یکایک سلیح' ('one-by-one weapons' / 'each and every weapon') is the object phrase and appears after the verb phrase, a normal order in older Persian.
segments.s240-g236: که این کار بر ما گذشت از مزیح begins with 'که' giving a causal/explanatory link ('for/because'). 'این کار' = 'this deed/affair' is the subject of 'گذشت' (passed/happened/befell), and 'بر ما' marks the experiencer ('upon us'). 'از مزیح' ('from jest/mockery') indicates the manner or character — i.e. that the event came as a mockery of us.
segments.s240-g237: چنین زار گشتیم و خوار و زبون is a predicate cluster describing the speakers' state. 'چنین' = 'thus/so,' followed by three adjectives linked to the first-person plural copular verb 'گشتیم' ('we became'). The adjectives 'زار', 'خوار', 'زبون' are listed in series (wretched / despised / abject), a common rhetorical piling in epic diction.
segments.s240-g238: که یک تن سوی ما گراید به خون is a result or consequence clause introduced by 'که' ('that/so that'). 'یک تن' = 'one person' is the subject, 'سوی ما گراید' = 'turns toward us' (سوی = toward; گراید is present/habitual 'turns/comes'), and 'به خون' ('for blood' / 'with bloodshed') states purpose or intent — i.e. he comes with the aim of bloodshed.
segments.s240-g239: همی بفگند نام مردی ز ما combines the adverb 'همی' (a frequentative/continuative 'continually' or 'ever') with the verb 'بفگند' (to cast/scatter). 'نام مردی ز ما' ('the name/fame of a man from us') is the object; the construction says they will keep throwing away or scattering that man's reputation among us.
segments.s240-g240: به تیغ او براند ز خون آسیا uses the instrumental 'به' ('by/with') + 'تیغ او' ('his sword') to mark the means. 'براند' (3rd sg.) is a transitive verb 'to drive'/'to put to flight' — here meaning he will drive (them/us) by that sword. 'ز خون آسیا' is a poetic genitival/figurative phrase: 'the mill of blood' (آسیا = mill), describing the bloody result or the place to which they will be driven; word order places the instrument and actor before the verb, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s240-g225: یکایک is an adverbial compound meaning 'one by one / each in turn' and modifies the whole clause. خروشیدن here is a deverbal noun/infinitive meaning 'roaring/crying' that functions as the event; آمد is the past tense verb 'came' (literal word order: event-word + آمد = 'a roar came'). ز + دشت is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the plain.'
segments.s240-g226: همه اسپ is the subject phrase 'all the horses.' یک بر دگر is an adverbial formula meaning 'one upon another / one after another.' برگذشت is a past-tense verb (compound in form) meaning 'passed by' or 'surged past' — so literal ordering is Subject + adverbial phrase + verb: 'all the horses one after another passed.'
segments.s240-g227: ز introduces the source 'from.' خاک پی رخش is a genitive chain: خاک ('dust') + پی ('track; trace') + رخش ('his steed'), so 'the dust of the steed's trail.' بر is a preposition 'upon/on' and سرکشان is a plural participial/adjectival form (literary) referring to 'those tossing their heads/proud ones' — here it modifies the location/people on whom the dust fell.
segments.s240-g228: پدید آمد is a two-word verbal expression meaning 'appeared' (پدید as 'manifest/appear' + آمد 'came' used as light verb). از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar / at a distance.' پیدا is an adjective 'visible, apparent' modifying نشان ('sign/mark'), so the clause reads 'a visible sign appeared from afar.'
segments.s240-g229: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when.' چوپان is the subject 'the shepherd.' بر + شاه توران is a classical prepositional phrase meaning 'to/toward the king of Turan' (بر used with رسید to indicate reaching someone). رسید is the past tense 'reached/arrived.'
segments.s240-g230: بدو is 'to him' (indirect object: 'told him'). باز here means 'back / again' and in this idiom (باز گفت) conveys 'returned and told' or 'went back and related.' گفت is the past of گفتن 'said/told' (the provided sense list does not give the quoted verb sense). آن شگفتی is 'that marvel/wonder,' and که دید forms a relative clause 'which he saw' (که + past verb = 'that he saw').
segments.s240-g231: که introduces reported content ('that...'). تنها is an adverb/adjective 'alone/only' modifying the agent. گله is 'flock/herd' (the object taken). برد here is intended as the past of بردن 'carried/took/drove away' (the provided noun senses do not match this verbal use). رستم is the agent (proper name). ز دشت is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain.'
segments.s240-g232: ز ما is literally 'from us' and here means 'of us' (indicating victims). کشت is the verb 'killed' (past of کشتن) — the provided senses include 'killing' which matches the verbal sense. بسیار modifies کشت as 'many / a great many.' و اندر گذشت links the clause with 'and' + اندر گذشت meaning 'and he went on / passed on' (گذشت = 'passed' used intransitively; اندر is a literary locative/particle 'among/in' combining with گذشت in the idiom).
segments.s240-g41: بیاراست is a past, 3rd-person singular verbal form from آراستن ('to adorn, make beautiful'), placed before its object (گلشن). گلشن is the direct object 'garden'. بسان بهار is a simile ('like/as spring') postposed to describe how the garden was dressed; بسان = 'like' and بهار = 'spring (season)'.
segments.s240-g42: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/elders'. نشستند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb 'they sat' (here the provided senses list did not include the correct sense, so the appropriate sense index is unavailable). با شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'with the king' (با = with, شهریار = king) attached to the verb.
segments.s240-g43: چو is used repeatedly as a simile marker 'like/as' introducing a list of names (گودرز, رستم, گستهم). The clause is nominal and verbless (a poetic apposition), with و coordinating the names. گستهم here functions as a proper name (the provided sense did not match that use).
segments.s240-g44: چو again introduces another simile phrase. برزین and گرشاسپ are personal names in apposition. از تخم جم expresses origin/lineage ('from the seed/stock of Jam'), where از marks origin and تخم is used metaphorically for 'lineage' or 'seed'.
segments.s240-g45: چو introduces names as similes ('like Giv and like Roham'). کار‌آزمای is a compound: کار 'deed/action' plus آزمای (from آزما, 'to test'), forming an adjectival phrase 'tested/proven in deeds' or 'tried in action'. The words are juxtaposed without an explicit copula, a typical poetic construction.
segments.s240-g46: چو again marks a simile. گرگین is a proper name (Gorgin). خراد here functions as 'wise' (or a wise person) and is followed by فرخنده رای, a descriptive compound: فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate' + رای 'counsel/opinion', together meaning 'of auspicious/fortunate counsel'. The phrase characterizes the second pair's counsels.
segments.s240-g47: چو here is temporal 'when'. از روز یک ساعت اندر گذشت is a temporal phrase: از marks the portion 'of the day' (a partitive use), یک ساعت 'one hour', اندر 'in/within', and گذشت is past tense 'passed' — together 'when one hour of the day had passed'.
segments.s240-g48: بیامد is a past, 3rd-person-singular verb 'came'. بدرگاه is a locative/directional compound meaning 'into the royal presence/court'. چوپان is the subject 'shepherd', and ز دشت is an origin phrase 'from the plain' (ز used poetically for 'from'). Word order is literary (verb before its subject and complements).
segments.s240-g377: Literal order is poetic: numeral + distance, prepositional phrase, verb, then subject. دو فرسنگ با او = 'two farsangs (away) with him' = 'within two farsangs of him'; بشد is poetic past of شدن meaning 'came to be / came' and here places the action before naming the subject شهریار (the king) which follows the verb, a common inverted order in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s240-g378: بپدرود کردن is an infinitival/verbal-noun construction with the prefixed poetic ب- introducing the action 'to bid farewell'; کردن is the light/auxiliary verb. گرفت(ـش) is a past simple 'he took/ seized (him)' with the clitic ـش marking the object 'him', and کنار is an adverbial/locative 'aside/beside'. Together the phrase expresses purpose: 'took him aside in order to bid farewell.'
segments.s240-g379: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. با راه رستم is a prepositional phrase 'along/with Rostam's path/route'. هم‌آواز is an adverb/adjective 'in unison / in accord', and گشت is past of گشتن 'became / came to be' — here meaning 'fell into (the same way) / joined'. The whole segment means 'when he came into accord with (followed) Rostam's road.'
segments.s240-g380: سپهدار ایران is the compound subject 'the commander of Iran' (title + domain). ازو is the prepositional pronoun 'from him/it', and بازگشت is the past verb 'returned/withdrew'. Word order here places the verb after the prepositional pronoun, so the line reads 'the commander of Iran returned from him.'
segments.s240-g381: جهان پاک is 'the pure world' (noun + adjective). بر مهر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon/to his affection/favor'. گشت is the past of شدن/گشتن 'became/turned', and راست functions as a predicate/adverb 'straight/true'. The phrase literally says 'the pure world turned straight upon his favor.'
segments.s240-g382: همی داشت is a habitual/progressive past sense 'kept on having / continually gave'. گیتی is 'the world' as subject; بر انسان = 'toward/for man' (prepositional), که is the relative 'that/which', and خواست is the past of خواستن 'wanted/desired'. Together: 'the world continually gave to mankind that which it desired/willed.'
segments.s240-g383: برین گونه = بر + این گونه 'in this manner / thus'; گردد (گردد/گشت) is a present/subjunctive/habitual-form 'becomes / turns / happens' with همی marking habitual or continuous action. چرخ پیر (literally 'old wheel' = the aged heaven/fate) is the subject placed after the verb in poetic inversion: 'thus the old wheel/wheel of fate ever turns.'
segments.s240-g384: گهی ... گاهی gives alternating adverbial sense 'at times ... at other times'. چون کمانست = 'is like a bow' (چون = like; کمانست = 'is a bow'); و = 'and'; چو تیر = 'like an arrow'. The segment describes the wheel/sky as changing shape: sometimes bow-like, sometimes arrow-like.
segments.s240-g105: به آبش گرفت آرزو هم به نان — Word order is poetic: the verb phrase گرفت (took/was seized by) governs آرزو (desire/longing). The prepositional phrase به آبش ('for/at its water') shows the object of that longing: literally 'a longing took him for its water.' هم به نان adds 'and likewise for bread.' The clitic -ش on آب (آبش) is a possessive/relational suffix 'its/his,' so 'its water' or 'water for him.'
segments.s240-g106: سر از خواب بر کوهه زین زنان — سر از خواب بر is an idiomatic sequence 'lifted/raised his head from sleep' (سر = head; از خواب = from sleep; بر = up/on). کوهه is a locative 'on the hill/mount.' زین زنان is a verbal-noun phrase: زین (saddle) plus زنان, an action/agentive form meaning 'putting/saddling' (poetic/archaic usage), so the whole clause means he lifted his head from sleep and put/settled the saddle on (or took to) the hill. Word order places the circumstance (from sleep) before the motion/result.
segments.s240-g107: چو بگرفتش از آب روشن شتاب — چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' بگرفتش is the past-tense, prefixed imperfective/perfectic poetic form of گرفتن with the enclitic -ش 'it/him' (here 'he took it' or 'he seized it'). از آب روشن = 'from the clear/bright water' (آب روشن = clear water). شتاب at the end functions adverbially 'with haste' (typical poetic placement after the clause).
segments.s240-g108: به پیش آمدش چشمه چون گلاب — به پیش آمدش is 'came up/before him' (آمدش = came to/at him; به پیش = forward/before). چشمه is the subject 'a spring' (source of water). چون گلاب is a simile 'like rosewater' (چون = like; گلاب = rosewater), placed after the noun it modifies in poetic order.
segments.s240-g109: فرود آمد و رخش را آب داد — فرود آمد is the past tense 'he alighted/dismounted' (compound verb: فرود = descent + آمد = came). و connects the next action. رخش را is the definite object 'his steed' (را marks definite object). آب داد is 'gave water' (داد = gave; here a simple perfective past). The sequence describes two consecutive actions: dismounting and watering the horse.
segments.s240-g110: هم از ماندگی چشم را خواب داد — هم 'also' links this result to the previous clause. از ماندگی is a causative/ablative phrase 'from (or because of) lingering/exhaustion' (ماندگی here = remaining/continuance = weariness from long travel). چشم را is the definite object 'his eyes' (را definite marker). خواب داد literally 'gave sleep' = 'he let his eyes have sleep' i.e. he fell asleep or sleep came to his eyes. Word order places cause before the object and result in typical Persian sequence.
segments.s240-g111: کمندش ببازوی و ببر بیان — کمندش is 'his lasso' (کمند + possessive -ش). ببازوی is the prepositional/locative phrase ب + بازوی 'on/at the arm' (poetic fused form meaning 'on his arm'). و joins the next action. ببر is an archaic/poetic verbal form (here a form of بَردن/بَر) meaning 'he took/bore' or 'he carried,' and بیان in this context is the object being taken/handled (here likely a strap/bridle or part of the gear); in poetry some nouns acquire specialized senses. Overall the clause says he put his lasso on his arm and took up the (bridle/gear).
segments.s240-g112: بپوشیده و تنگ بسته میان — بپوشیده is a past participial/perfect form 'having covered' or 'covered/wrapped' (poetic prefix ب-). و links the adjectives/actions. تنگ بسته = 'tight-bound' (تنگ = tight/close; بسته = tied/fastened). میان here is a locative/relational adverb 'in the middle' or 'between' (specifies where it is bound). The whole describes how the gear/rope was wrapped and tightly fastened in the middle.
segments.s240-g129: سوی آبت اندازم ار سوی کوه combines a directional phrase + verb and an immediate conditional 'if' clause. سوی آبت (سوی + آبت) = 'toward your water' with ـت as the archaic 2nd-person possessive suffix on آب. اندازم is a 1st-person form (classical/subjunctive/optative) of انداختن 'to throw/cast' used here like 'I will cast' or 'I cast'. ار introduces the conditional 'if', and سوی کوه repeats the directional idea 'toward the mountain'. Word order places the direction before the verb, and the conditional particle links the two possibilities.
segments.s240-g130: کجا خواهی افتاد دور از گروه is a question about the result of the conditional. کجا fronted = 'where'. خواهی + افتاد is the classical/periphrastic future: خواهی (future auxiliary 'you will') + past-stem افتاد yields 'you will fall'. دور از گروه is an adverbial phrase 'far from the company/troop' (دور acting adverbially, از marking separation).
segments.s240-g131: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. رستم is the subject. بگفتار او is an oblique phrase 'according to/at his words; listening to him' (ب- prefixed to گفتار to mark 'with regard to speech'), and بنگرید is the classical perfect/past form of نگر (he looked/turned his gaze). The verb comes at the end, following classical Persian word order.
segments.s240-g132: هوا در کف دیو واژونه دید is a small clause headed by دید 'saw'. هوا here means 'air' (the element/wind). در کف دیو = 'in the demon's palm' (در + کف with diو as possessor), واژونه is a predicate adjective 'upside-down/overturned' describing that air. The whole structure: 'he saw the air (هوا) overturned (واژونه) in the demon's palm.'
segments.s240-g133: چنین 'thus/so' is an adverb introducing reported speech. گفت here functions as 'said' (classical past), با خویشتن = 'to/with himself' (a reflexive expression), and پیلتن is the name of the speaker placed after the phrase for emphasis — so: 'Thus Piltan said to himself.'
segments.s240-g134: که introduces the content of his thought/speech ('that' / subordinate clause). بد + نامبردار: بد negates or gives a negative tone to نامبردار (here used in the sense 'named/known' or 'reputed'), producing 'ill‑reputed' / 'badly named' — i.e. 'bear a bad name.' هر انجمن = 'in every assembly/every gathering' (هر qualifying انجمن). The clause is elliptical (subject/pronoun is implied): '(that) I may not be ill‑reputed in any company.'
segments.s240-g135: گر is the conditional 'if'. اندازدم is 1st-person form 'I throw/cast' (classical subjunctive/optative sense 'if I cast'). گفت is parenthetical 'he said' inserted after the condition-starter, and بر کوهسار = 'upon the mountainside' (بر + کوهسار). The sequence shows the speaker imagining the conditional action and reporting it immediately ('If I cast (him) — he said — upon the mountain').
segments.s240-g136: تن و استخوانم 'my body and my bones' uses the possessive enclitic ـم on استخوان to mean 'my bones' while تن ('body') is coordinated with و. نیاید is a negative form 'will not come/appear' used here idiomatically with بکار to mean 'will not be of use' or 'will be useless'. بکار literally 'to use' functions as a predicative complement: 'will not come to use' = 'will not be of use.'
segments.s240-g113: ز (preposition) here introduces origin/partitive 'from' or marks the object whose strap is being acted on; زین 'saddle' is the direct noun. کیانیش is کیان + the possessive enclitic -ش (his/of the Kian), so 'the Kian's (saddle)'. بگشاد is ب- + گشاد (past/preterite) meaning 'he loosened/unfastened' (the b- here is the old narrative past-marker rather than the adjectival sense). تنگ is used as 'tight'/'girth' (the tight fastening) — syntactically the object being opened/loosened.
segments.s240-g114: به بالین نهاد is a verb phrase 'placed/put (on/by) the pillow': به marks direction/place ('on/at'), بالین is 'pillow/at the bedside', and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/put'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', and جناغ خدنگ is a nominal phrase referring to the blade/dagger (جناغ here functioning as part of the noun phrase with خدنگ); together they form the direct object 'that dagger' which he set by the pillow.
segments.s240-g115: چراگاه is a locative 'pasture/feeding-place'; رخش is the steed's name (the horse Rakhsh) used as subject; آمد is the past 'came'. و links the two actions/phrases. جای خواب is a genitive/compound 'the place of sleeping' ('at the sleeping-place') and serves as the setting for the following verb.
segments.s240-g116: نمدزین is a compound adjective/noun 'felt-covered / felt-saddle' describing what is thrown off. برافگند is the past 'he cast off / threw away' (بـ- prefix here forms the past narrative). بر پیش آب is a directional/locative phrase: بر = 'onto/toward', پیش = 'before/in front of', آب = 'water', so 'threw it off toward/in front of the water'.
segments.s240-g117: بدان جایگه is a frozen locative/demonstrative 'in that place' (بدان = به + آن). خفت is the past 'he lay down/slept'. و links the clauses; خوابش ربود is a common poetic phrase 'sleep seized him' (خواب + the enclitic -ش 'his', ربود = 'seized/took'), i.e., 'and slumber took him.'
segments.s240-g118: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause 'because/for'. از رنج وز تاختن مانده بود literally 'he had remained from the hardship/burden of galloping': از = 'from/because of', رنج = 'toil/suffering', وز here means 'burden/weight' (so 'toil and burden'), تاختن = infinitive 'galloping/charging', مانده بود is the past perfect-ish construction 'had remained/was left' indicating he was left (i.e., exhausted) by that toil.
segments.s240-g119: چو is the temporal 'when'. اکوانش appears as a proper-name with enclitic -ش (Akvan + -sh) meaning 'Akvan (or his)'; here it functions as the subject 'Akvan (he)'. از دور = 'from afar', خفته = 'having slept/asleep' (adjectival/participial), and بدید is the past 'saw'. The clause therefore reads 'When Akvan saw him sleeping from a distance...'.
segments.s240-g120: یکی here means 'one / a certain' (indefinite subject). باد شد is a poetic phrase 'became wind / a wind arose' (باد = 'wind', شد = 'became/rose'). تا introduces purpose/resultive motion 'until/so that' but functions here to link to the following verb; بر او رسید is 'reached him' (بر = 'to/upon', او = 'him', رسید = 'arrived/reached'). Together: 'a (one) became wind and reached him.'
segments.s240-g33: خردمند is the head noun ('a wise man'); کاین is an older fused demonstrative/relative (here 'this/that') introducing the following nominal phrase; داستان is the object 'tale/story'; بشنود is 3rd-person singular classical present/subjunctive of شنیدن used in a relative clause ('who hears' / 'who may hear'). Word order: head noun + relative clause.
segments.s240-g34: بدانش is a compound adverbial/phrase meaning 'to that' or 'toward that' (بدان + ش as a bound form); گراید is the classical verb meaning 'turns/leans' (3rd-person, here optative/resultive sense); بدین is 'to this' (prepositional/directional), دین = 'religion/faith'; نگرود is the classical form of گرویدن 'to adopt/embrace' (here complementary infinitival idea). Together the clause says that his understanding inclines and he embraces this faith.
segments.s240-g35: ولیکن is the concessive adversative 'but'; چو here is temporal/conjunctive 'when'; معنیش is معن + possessive -ش 'its meaning'; یادآوری is a deverbal noun 'recollection/recalling'. The phrase functions as 'but when its meaning is brought to mind'.
segments.s240-g36: شود is the stative/resultive third-person form of شدن 'become'; رام is an adjectival predicate 'tame/soft'; و joins the next clause; کوته is adjective 'short' and کند is the verb 'makes/does' (here causative/result: 'renders/shortens'); داوری is the object 'judgment'. The construction says the (effect) becomes mild and makes judgment brief.
segments.s240-g37: تو is the explicit 2nd-person pronoun 'you' (poetic/thou); بشنو is the 2nd-person imperative of شنیدن 'listen'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/from the words of'; گفتار is 'speech/words'; دهقان پیر is a genitival noun phrase 'the old dehqan' (دهقان often denotes the landed gentry/storyteller here). The whole segment is an imperative instruction: 'You, listen to the words of the old dehqan.'
segments.s240-g38: گر is the conditional 'if'; ایدونک is a modal/uncertainty particle (classical/poetic) implying 'perchance' or 'if so'; باشد is 3rd-person subjunctive/potential of بودن 'be' ('if it be'); سخن دلپذیر is a nominal predicate 'the speech (is) pleasing'. The clause expresses a conditional/hypothetical: 'if perhaps the speech is pleasing.'
segments.s240-g39: سخنگوی دهقان is a genitive phrase 'the spokesman/speaker of the dehqan' (سخنگو used as agent); چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; کرد is the past tense of کردن 'did/made' used here with یاد as its object; یاد is a noun 'remembrance/recall'. The phrase means 'the dehqan's speaker thus recalled/said'.
segments.s240-g40: که introduces the reported/complement clause 'that ...'; یک روز is the temporal expression 'one day'; کیخسرو is the subject/name 'Kay Khosrow'; از بامداد is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'from dawn' (i.e., at daybreak). This begins the narrated event: 'that one day Kay Khosrow, from the dawn, ...'.
segments.s240-g361: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); کین سیاوش is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the vengeance of Siavash' (noun + name). به here marks the target/recipient ('to/onto' or 'against') and precedes the objects پیل و گله ('elephants and herds'). Word order is literary: relative introducer + possession + prepositional phrase indicating what the vengeance would be directed toward.
segments.s240-g362: نشاید is a fixed literary impersonal/forms meaning 'it is not fitting' (negative deontic). چنین 'thus/so' modifies the following verbal phrase. خوار کردن is a light-verb construction: خوار (despised/abased) + کردن 'to make', i.e. 'to abase/demean'. یله here is used adjectivally/nominally ('loose/unrestrained' / 'vile' in context) and completes the object of 'خوار کردن' ('to demean to a low/unrestrained state').
segments.s240-g363: در گنج is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the treasury/treasure-house'. بگشاد is an archaic narrative/past form of 'to open' (ب- prefix + گشودن) meaning 'he opened'. شاه جهان (literally 'king world') is the subject following the verb in poetic word order: 'the king of the world opened (it) in the treasury.'
segments.s240-g364: گرانمایه چیزی = 'a precious/valuable thing' (adjective + noun). که introduces a relative clause; بودش is the past copula with the enclitic pronoun -ش 'it', here 'it was'; نهان is the predicate adjective 'hidden'. Word order (بودش نهان) places the verb before the adjective in a compact relative clause: 'something precious that was hidden.'
segments.s240-g365: بیاورد is a third-person past/narrative form 'he brought' (common in epic narration). ده جام گوهر is a noun phrase with numeral + head noun + modifier: 'ten cups (جام) of jewels (گوهر)' or 'ten jewelled cups'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', governing گنج 'treasury'.
segments.s240-g366: بزر بافته جامه is an adjectival + participle + noun sequence: بزر 'great', بافته 'woven' (past participle used adjectivally), جامه 'garment/robe' — 'great woven garments'. شاه here marks possession/association 'of the king'. The numeral 'پنج' in the line supplies 'five' for these garments (poetic word order places the parts compactly).
segments.s240-g367: غلامان روزمی is a compound noun phrase 'the daily (or customary) servants/attendants' (غلامان + روزمی). بزرین qualifies کمر: بزرین کمر = 'great/large girdles' (adjective before noun in this poetic order). The segment lists attendants and their belts compactly, typical of epic enumeration where verbs may be omitted between noun phrases.
segments.s240-g368: پرستندگان نیز 'the worshippers/attendants also' — نیز 'also' links this group to the previous one. با + طوق زر is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with a collar (طوق) of gold (زر)'; طوق is a neck-collar/torque and زر specifies the material. The construction describes their adornment ('also with gold collars').
segments.s240-g89: 'بدانست' is a past simple verb 'he realized/knew' placed before the subject (verb–subject order is common in epic poetry). 'که' introduces the subordinate clause 'that…'; 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that'; 'نیست' is the negative copula 'is not/does not exist' here functioning as 'is not (so, will not be so)'; 'گور' is the object of the assertion (here the name/animal gūr), so the clause says 'that that gūr is not (so/will not be so)'.
segments.s240-g90: 'ابا' is a verbal noun/verb meaning 'to refuse / be reluctant' and here indicates that the gūr (او) resists; 'او' refers back to that gūr; 'کنون' = 'now'; 'چاره' = 'a remedy/means'; 'باید' is the impersonal modal 'it is necessary/one must'; the negator 'نه' before 'زور' negates the method, so 'not force' — the line says a remedy is needed now, not by force.
segments.s240-g91: 'جز' introduces an exception ('except'); 'اکوان' here is a proper name (Akvān, the demon) rather than the common noun 'worlds' and so does not match the ordinary senses given; 'دیو' = 'demon'; 'این' = 'this'; 'نشاید' is an impersonal negative 'it is not fitting/possible'; 'بدن' = 'the body' — together: 'Except Akvān the demon, it is not fitting/possible for anyone to (affect) this body.'
segments.s240-g92: 'ببایستش' is the necessity verb 'it must (be done to) it' with the enclitic -ش = 'it' (the body); 'از' = 'by/with' or 'by means of'; 'باد' literally 'wind' (here a simile: a swift motion like the wind); 'تیغی' is adjectival/ Verbal form related to 'sword' (a sword‑blow); 'زدن' = 'to strike' — so literally 'it must be struck by a sword‑blow like the wind.'
segments.s240-g93: 'بشمشیر' (ب + شمشیر) is an instrumental phrase 'with/by the sword' used adverbially; 'باید' is the impersonal modal 'must/it is necessary'; 'کنون' = 'now'; 'چاره' = 'remedy/means'; 'کرد' is the past root 'do' used here to mean 'take (that) measure' — together: 'One must now, by the sword, take a remedy.'
segments.s240-g94: 'دوانیدن' is a causative verb 'to make run' (here 'to cause to flow'); 'خون' = 'blood' (object); 'بران' = contraction of 'بر آن' = 'upon that/thereon'; 'چرم' = 'hide/leather'; 'زرد' = 'yellow' (adjective). Grammatical order: verbal phrase + object + prepositional phrase = 'to make blood run upon that yellow hide.'
segments.s240-g95: 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' = 'from'; 'دانا' = 'a wise (man)'; 'شنیدم' verb, 1st person past 'I heard' (here 'I was told'); 'که' introduces the reported clause; 'این جای اوست' = 'this is his place' with 'ست' = copula 'is' (poetic/clipped form of 'است').
segments.s240-g96: 'که' = 'that' (reportive). 'گفتند' here is the common narrative 'they said' (note: the provided sense list did not include the verbal 'they said', so sense is marked -1). 'بستاند' in this context means 'take/strip off' (they say he takes/strips); the provided senses do not include this meaning, so marked -1. 'از' = 'from'; 'گور' = gūr (here the animal/name whose hide is taken); 'پوست' = 'skin/hide' — the clause reports hearsay that he strips the hide from the gūr.
segments.s240-g177: بدان is a contracted locative (به + آن) meaning 'to/at that'; the clause begins with this locative phrase followed by the noun چشمه ('spring') and the verb آمد ('came')—word order is locative + noun + verb (poetic inversion). کجا introduces a relative/locative 'where' clause modifying 'چشمه'. خفته بود is a past-state predicate: خفته (asleep) + بود (past 'was') indicating he had been sleeping.
segments.s240-g178: بران is a locative adverb ('thereon/at that') linking back to the spring. دیو بدگوهر is a noun phrase with an epithet: دیو 'demon' modified by بدگوهر 'ill‑favored/vile'; this whole phrase is the subject. آشفته بود is the predicate (آشفته 'disturbed/agitated' + بود past), so syntactically Subject → Predicate, with بران giving the precise location.
segments.s240-g179: نبود is the past negative 'was not' placed sentence-initially (poetic inversion). رخش رخشان is an appositional pair: the proper name/horse رخش with the adjective رخشان 'radiant/bright'. بران مرغزار is a locative phrase ('in that meadow'), with بران (= بر + آن) marking 'there/on that'.
segments.s240-g180: جهانجوی is the noun/epithet 'world‑seeking' (a person seeking fame/world). شد is past 'became'. تند is a predicate adjective 'swift/impetuous'. با روزگار is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with fate/the times'—together the clause says the world‑seeker became impetuous under the pressure/influence of fate.
segments.s240-g181: برآشفت is a poetic past form meaning 'he became enraged / rose up agitated'. و is the conjunction 'and'. برداشت is past of برداشتن 'took/removed' used transitively here, with its objects زین 'saddle' and لگام 'bridle' following—verb then objects (common in poetry).
segments.s240-g182: بشد is past of شدن here meaning roughly 'it came to pass / he set out' (or 'he mounted'). بر پی رخش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in pursuit of Rakhsh' (بر + پی, with پی = 'in pursuit of/after'). تا گاه شام sets the extent in time: 'until the hour/evening (شام)'.
segments.s240-g183: پیاده is an adverbial 'on foot'. همی is an archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or habitual action (here 'were/kept'). رفت is the past 'went'. جویان شکار is a compound noun phrase 'seekers of prey' (i.e., hunters) where جویان = 'seekers' and شکار = 'hunt/prey'.
segments.s240-g184: به پیش 'forward/ahead' gives direction. اندر آمد is the verb phrase 'came into view/entered among' (اندر آمد = 'appeared/entered'). یکی مرغزار is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain meadow' (یکی = 'one/a certain', مرغزار = 'meadow'), so the whole clause means 'ahead came a meadow'.
segments.s240-g73: The time phrase 'سه روزش' (literally 'three days-his') gives duration ('for three days'); in classical Persian the clitic -ش can mark a durational/possessive sense and follows the numeral. 'همی' is the continuous/progressive particle marking repeated or ongoing action, and it combines with the past verb 'جست' here in the sense 'was seeking/kept seeking' (not 'to leap'). The locative phrase 'در مرغزار' ('in the meadow') follows the verb and indicates where the seeking/hunting took place.
segments.s240-g74: 'همی کرد' is another continuous/periphrastic marker: 'همی' (ongoing) + 'کرد' (to do) forms the verbal phrase 'kept doing' or idiomatically 'kept hunting' when paired with the noun 'شکار'. 'بر گرد اسپان' is a prepositional/locative cluster meaning 'around the horses' (بر + گرد = 'around/about' plus 'اسپان' horses). Word order places the surrounding phrase before the object noun 'شکار', yielding 'kept (doing) hunt around the horses.'
segments.s240-g75: 'چهارم' ('on the fourth') gives the temporal point. 'بدیدش' is the past verb 'saw' with the enclitic object -ش (here referring to the boars); classical word order allows the verb (with enclitic) to appear before the noun phrase. 'گرازان بدشت' is the noun phrase 'boars on the plain' (بدشت = 'on/in the plain').
segments.s240-g76: This is a simile introduced by 'چو' meaning 'as'/'like'. 'باد شمالی' is the image 'northern wind'. The verbal cluster 'برو بر گذشت' describes swift passing: 'برو' ('went/ran'), 'بر' (poetic preposition 'over/along'), and 'گذشت' ('passed'). Together they mean 'they/it passed by like a northern wind.'
segments.s240-g77: 'درخشنده زرین' are stacked adjectives 'shining, golden' placed before the noun phrase. 'یکی' ('one, a certain') qualifies 'باره' here as 'one piece/one instance' (an idiomatic way to introduce a single object), and 'بود' is the past copula 'there was.' The sequence therefore states the existence of one shining golden item.
segments.s240-g78: This clause describes what was inside that object. 'بچرم' should be read as 'به چرم' (made of leather) or otherwise indicates material (here treated as an attributive), 'اندرون' = 'inside/within', 'زشت' = 'ugly', and 'پتیاره' is an archaic/rare noun for a small container or pouch; 'بود' is the copula 'there was.' The clause therefore means 'inside was an ugly (leather) pouch/box.' (Note: 'بچرم' is archaic/ambiguous in the lemma list and is treated here as a material-marking phrase.)
segments.s240-g79: 'برانگیخت' is the past transitive 'roused/awoke' and takes 'رخش' (the famous steed, 'Rakhsh') as its object. 'دلاور' ('the brave one') is appositive/epithet referring to the rider (or hero) associated with the action. 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from' and 'جای' = 'place.' The clause reads 'the brave (man) roused Rakhsh from place.'
segments.s240-g80: 'چو' here means 'when.' 'تنگ اندر آمد' = 'entered/was drawn into a narrow place' (تنگ = narrow, اندر آمد = came/entered in). 'دگر شد' literally 'became other/again' = 'changed/turned again' or 'became ready once more.' The final word 'برای' is polyvalent (literally 'for'/'because of'/'in order to') and in this poetic line serves to mark purpose or orientation; the whole clause conveys that upon entering the narrow spot the steed changed its posture/attitude (turned/ready) — a somewhat idiomatic description of the horse's movement in battle.
segments.s240-g305: از + ایدر = a locative start 'from here'; میان را is the direct object (را marks 'the middle'). بدان is an archaic demonstrative/compound (بِ+آن) meaning 'with that / into that', and کرد بند literally 'made a bond/tied' (کرد = did/made; بند = fetter/band). The verb-then-subject/phrase order is poetic: he 'made/tied' the middle 'with that'.
segments.s240-g306: کجا = 'where'; گور here is the animal 'onager' (wild ass) rather than 'grave'; گیرد is used in the sense 'is/gets caught' (passive sense); بخم کمند = 'in the curve/coil of the snare' (بخم an archaic/discursive word qualifying the shape, کمند = lasso/snare). Together: 'where the onager is caught in the snare's loop.'
segments.s240-g307: کنون = 'now'; دیو و پیل lists two adversaries 'demon and elephant'; آمدستش is a fused archaic form meaning 'befell him / came upon him' (آمد + ـست + اش), and بچنگ is a prepositional compound 'into the grasp' (ب + چنگ = into the grip). The clause says these foes fell upon him and seized him.
segments.s240-g308: بخشکی = 'on the dry land' (locative use of a word related to 'dry/land'); پلنگ names the land predator 'panther/leopard'; و links to the next; بدریا = 'into the sea / in the sea' and نهنگ = 'whale'. The short clause contrasts the foes by domain: on land the panther, in the sea the whale.
segments.s240-g309: نیابد is an archaic negative form meaning 'does not find / cannot find'; گذر = 'passage' and شیر = 'lion' (subject of the clause in sense 'no lion'); بر تیغ اوی = 'before/against his sword' (بر = upon/against, تیغ = sword, اوی = his). Syntax: 'No lion finds a passage against his sword.'
segments.s240-g310: همان = 'that same / the very one'; دیو = 'demon'; و هم = 'and also'; مردم = 'people'; کینه‌جوی = adjective 'vengeful / revenge-seeking'. This noun phrase picks up enemies: 'that demon and also the revenge-seeking people.'
segments.s240-g311: پذیره شدن is a verbal-noun phrase 'the being received / the reception'; را marks it as the object; بیاراست is a past/poetic verb 'adorned / prepared'; شاه follows as the subject in inverted poetic order. Literal: 'The king prepared/adorned the reception.'
segments.s240-g312: بسر (= به سر) + بر نهادند = 'they put on (upon the head)' — a common idiom 'to put on the head' meaning to don a helmet or cap; گردان here denotes the troop/contingent doing the action and کلاه = 'hat/helmet'. So: 'the ranks (troop) put helmets on their heads.' The verb is plural (نهادند), matching 'گردان'.
segments.s240-g25: شگفتست is the poetic contraction of شگفت است (is wondrous). The clause follows a subject + predicate order (جهان … شگفتست). چون introduces a subordinate conditional/adverbial clause 'as/when', and بنگری is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/oblique present (بِنگَری) meaning 'if/when you look'.
segments.s240-g26: ندارد is the negative present of داشتن (does not have); Persian allows the verb to appear before the indefinite subject that follows it. کسی after the verb here, with the negation, yields the sense 'no one'. آلت داوری is a noun phrase 'the instrument/means of judgment' (آلت = means, داوری = judgment).
segments.s240-g27: که here introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). جانت is the possessive form 'your soul' (the -ت enclitic); شگفتست repeats the predicate 'is wondrous'. و connects the two coordinated nouns (جانت … و تن), and هم marks 'also' for تن (body). شگفت here is an adjective 'wondrous'.
segments.s240-g28: نخست ('first') is a sentence adverb marking priority. از خود اندازه is an idiomatic phrase 'measure from/yourself' (i.e. 'measure yourself'); اندازه is the noun 'measure'. باید + گرفت is an impersonal necessity construction: باید (it is necessary / one must) + گرفت (infinitival/past-stem form of گرفتن) = 'one must take' or 'it must be taken'.
segments.s240-g29: دگر means 'other' / 'another' (here introducing a contrasting point). آنک is a relative/demonstrative 'that which / that', and این is the demonstrative 'this'. گرد گردان is a poetic doublet: گرد (relating to turning/going about) combined with گردان ('revolving') modifies سپهر ('sky, heavens'), yielding 'this revolving sky'. Word order places the adjectives before the noun in classic style.
segments.s240-g30: همی is a habitual/continuous adverb 'always' or 'ever'. نو is the adjective 'new' modifying چهر (face/appearance). نمایدت is an archaic literary 3rd-person singular present + 2nd-person singular object enclitic (-ت): 'it shows you'. هر روز (every day) is a time expression placed before the object (چهر). The whole means 'it continually shows you a new face each day.'
segments.s240-g31: نباشی is a negative jussive/imperative form 'do not be' (classical usage). بدین is the prepositional phrase به + این ('with/to this' or 'about this'); here it's written as one unit in the text (the provided segmentation splits it, but the meaning is 'with this saying'). گفته is 'saying/statement', and همداستان is an adjective meaning 'like‑minded / in agreement'. Together: 'Do not be in agreement with this statement.'
segments.s240-g32: که introduces the reported clause 'as/that'. دهقان is 'the peasant' (subject). همی is the habitual adverb 'always' and گوید is the literary present 'says' (here 'he says' or 'has been saying'); the corpus's listed senses for گوید do not include the verb sense, so note it functions as 'says'. از باستان is a temporal phrase 'from antiquity / since ancient times'. The clause thus reports a common rural saying: 'as the peasant always says from ancient times.'
segments.s240-g185: همه precedes the nouns to mean 'the whole' or 'all of'. بیشه and آبهای are coordinated nouns ('grove and waters'); روان is a postpositive adjective modifying آبهای (literally 'flowing waters'). In Persian poetic style adjectives often follow the noun they modify and the copula is omitted, so this phrase names the setting 'the entire grove and the flowing waters.'
segments.s240-g186: بهر جای here functions as 'as/for a place' (bahr + jāy) indicating what the grove and waters served as. دراج و قمری are coordinated nouns naming two kinds of birds; نوان is an adjective after them ('young'/'new'), following the usual Persian order where adjectives come after the noun. There is no explicit copula—this is a descriptive verbless construction common in poetry.
segments.s240-g187: This is a possessive/nominal phrase: گله‌دار ('herdsman') followed by اسپان افراسیاب, where اسپان افراسیاب = 'the horses of Afrasiab' (possessor follows the possessed noun in Persian nominal phrases). Together they mean 'the herdsman of Afrasiab's horses.'
segments.s240-g188: به + بیشه + درون gives a spatial phrase 'into/at the grove, inside'. سر نهاده is a light-verb construction (نهاده, past participle of نهاد 'to place') meaning 'having laid (his) head' or 'laid (his) head down'. بخواب here stands elliptically for بخواب رفت (to fall asleep) or simply 'to sleep/ fell asleep' — poetry often drops parts of light-verb phrases and auxiliary verbs.
segments.s240-g189: دمان رخش is a possessive noun phrase 'the dāman (flank/skirt) of Rakhsh' (Rakhsh is the horse's name). بر مادیانان is a prepositional phrase 'over/upon the mares.' چو دیو is a simile: چو = 'as/like' and دیو = 'a demon/monster', so the image is 'Rakhsh's flank/streamers rose over the mares like a demon.'
segments.s240-g190: میان گله is 'in the middle of the herd' (میان = middle/among, گله = herd). برکشیده is a past participle/preterital adjective 'raised' or 'lifted,' describing an action performed in that place; غریو is the sound noun 'a roar/cry' — together 'he raised a roar in the midst of the herd.'
segments.s240-g191: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The sequence is subject (رستم) + verb-with-enclitic (بدیدش = 'saw it' where -ش = 'it/him'). کیانی کمند ('Kayanian/Kiani lasso') is an object phrase placed here for emphasis; in poetic syntax direct objects or instruments can appear earlier and be linked to the following verb (بیفگند).
segments.s240-g192: بیفگند is a simple past 'he threw/cast' (third-person). The subject is Rostam from the previous clause. و is the conjunction 'and.' سرش is 'his/its head' (سر + enclitic ش). اندر آمد is 'came into' (اندر being an older form of در 'in'), and به بند = 'into the bond/fetter' (بند = fetter/noose). The whole clause means 'and its head came into the noose,' an idiom for being caught.
segments.s240-g321: وزانجا is a classical fused adverb meaning 'and/from there' (و + از آن‌جا) and functions as a temporal/locative adverb at the start of the clause. بایوان is an archaic/uncertain word here serving as the (plural) subject referring to the king's attendants/retainers; its plurality is shown by the verb آمدند which is 3rd‑person plural past. شاه follows as the possessor/title linked to those attendants (classical Persian often places such nouns adjacent without an explicit izafe in poetry). آمدند is a past plural intransitive verb 'they came.'
segments.s240-g322: گشاده دل و نیک‌خواه is a coordinated adjectival phrase modifying the (plural) subject; گشاده‌دل is a compound 'open‑hearted / generous' and works as a single descriptive unit, while نیک‌خواه ('well‑wishing, benevolent') is the second coordinated descriptor joined by و. آمدند repeats the past plural verb and agrees with the same subject; the two adjectives precede the verb (typical S‑Adj‑V ordering in Persian poetry).
segments.s240-g323: به ایرانیان is an indirect object marked by the preposition به ('to/for') and names the beneficiaries. بر گله here uses بر + گله to indicate the motive or object of appeasement — 'on/for (their) complaint' — so that the following بخش کرد means 'gave a gift/portion' to placate them; کرد is the light verb 'did/made' forming the verbal phrase 'بخش کرد' = 'bestowed / gave.' Word order places the beneficiary first and the causal phrase next (poetic order).
segments.s240-g324: نشست here is a past tense verb 'he sat down' (though نشست can also be a noun, the verbal sense is intended). تن خویشتن is an emphatic reflexive phrase 'his own body / himself' (تن = body, خویشتن = oneself). رخش کرد is a causative/result clause: رخش (the steed Rakhsh) is the object and کرد ('did/made') conveys that he caused or settled his horse (i.e. had his horse made to rest or secured it). The two verbs describe successive actions: he sat himself and he secured his steed.
segments.s240-g325: فرستاد is the past tense 'he sent / he dispatched.' پیلان is the plural direct object 'elephants.' بر introduces the target/direction ('toward/against') and is followed by پیل شاه — 'the king's elephant' (classical verse often omits an explicit izafe). The whole phrase describes sending elephants in relation to the royal elephant.
segments.s240-g326: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'). بر شیر here means 'against the lions' (بر + شیر), پیلان is the subject 'the elephants' again, and بگیرند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive of گرفتن 'to take/seize' (subjunctive used after که with a purposive/result meaning). راه is the object 'way / path,' so the clause says the elephants were to seize/secure the way against the lions.
segments.s240-g327: بیک هفته is a temporal adverbial ('in one week' / 'within a week') placed at the beginning to set the time span; this is an archaic classical expression. ایوان is the direct object 'the iwan / portico' and بیاراستند is the past plural verb 'they adorned / they arranged' (bi‑ + آراستن forming the past plural). The clause states that the iwan was decorated within the given time.
segments.s240-g328: می‌و رود is a coordinated pair meaning 'wine and (string) instrument' — می (wine) is here written as می‌و and رود is the musical 'rūd' (lute/string instrument) rather than 'river.' و links this list to رامشگران ('entertainers / peacemakers / comforters'), and خواستند is the past plural 'they requested / they summoned.' Together the sequence lists what they provided: wine, music, and performers.
segments.s240-g289: "چه گویی" is a rhetorical/questioning present-tense clause meaning "what do you say" or "how say you"; گویی here is the 2nd-person singular present of گفتن ("you say"). "تو‌ای" is the vocative "you, O" (poetic order), and "خواجه سالخورد" is a compound vocative: خواجه "master/ lord" modified by سالخورد (an adjective meaning "aged").
segments.s240-g290: چشیده is a past participle (literally "having tasted") used adjectivally/participially to mean "experienced." ز is the older poetic preposition "from/of," گیتی is "the world," بسی here intensifies ("much/very"). گرم و سرد is a correlative pair "warm and cold," i.e. the ups and downs or vicissitudes one has experienced.
segments.s240-g291: که داند is a relative/interrogative clause opener: که = "who/that" and داند is the 3rd-person singular present of دانستن ("knows"). The second که introduces the content clause ("that/ how"); چندین is an indefinite numeral/adjective "several / many a", and نشیب و فراز is a fixed pair meaning "descent(s) and ascent(s)" (i.e. lows and highs).
segments.s240-g292: به پیش آرد is a verbal phrase where آرد (3rd sing. present of آوردن) takes a prepositional phrase به پیش ("to the front / before us") and means "brings forward / will bring." این روزگار دراز is the direct object: این = "this," روزگار = "time/age/fate," دراز = "long."
segments.s240-g293: تگ روزگار is an attributive phrase: تگ (an older/poetic adjective meaning "narrow, constrained" or "single/pressed") modifies روزگار ("the age/fate"). از درازی که هست is a causal/relative phrase: از = "because of / from," درازی = "the length/duration," که هست = "that it has / that is," so the whole segment gives the cause or quality of the age ("the age's constriction because of its length").
segments.s240-g294: همی is an adverb meaning "continually / always." بگذراند is a causative/third-person present form of گذراندن here meaning "causes to pass" or "lets pass." سخنها is the plural of سخن ("words, talks, sayings"), and ز دست is a prepositional phrase literally "from the hand," an idiom meaning "out of one's hand / beyond one's control / slipping away." Together: "continually makes sayings slip from the hand."
segments.s240-g295: که داند repeats the interrogative/relative "who knows." کزین is the fused/prepositional form of از + این meaning "from this / of this." گنبد تیزگرد is a noun phrase: گنبد = "dome / vault (of heaven)" and تیزگرد is a poetic adjective "swift-turning / quickly revolving," placed after the noun in classical word order.
segments.s240-g296: درو here is best read as a contracted classical form (in context likely "در او" = "in it") or read along with سور as the pair در و سور ("doors and ramparts"); سور can mean "wall / rampart." چند است asks "how many are there" (چند = "how many," است = "is/are"). و چندی نبرد literally pairs that with "and how many (battles) [it] (takes/causes)" — نبرد in the lexicon can mean "battle/struggle," so the line rhetorically questions how many walls/feasts/battles are involved under the turning dome. (This segment preserves a dense, elliptical classical phrasing: the exact attachment of درو/دَر و is an orthographic/interpretive matter.)
segments.s240-g337: This is a simple nominal clause with two coordinated noun phrases describing appearance. دو چشمش = “his two eyes” (count + possessive enclitic). کبود is an adjectival predicate after the noun (post-nominal adjective, common in Persian poetry). و links the two descriptions; لبانش = “his lips” (plural noun + possessive enclitic) and سیاه is the adjective “black.” Word order is Noun + Adjective and the possessive -ش attaches to the noun.
segments.s240-g338: تنش را is the object phrase (تن “body” + possessive -ش; را marks it as definite/direct object or topicalized item). نشایست is an archaic negative/adversative form meaning “not fitting / unbecoming” (here built on شایسته but with negative n- prefix), and کردن + نگاه = the light-verb construction نگاه کردن “to look / to gaze.” The whole phrase literally reads “to look upon his body was not fitting,” with نشایست qualifying that the act of looking is improper.
segments.s240-g339: بدان here functions as a prepositional/compound sense “by/with that” (ب- + an older form), introducing the standard instrumental/causal frame. زور = “strength/might.” و = “and.” آن تن = “that body.” نباشد is the negative existential/impersonal form “there was not / there could not be” (used to state nonexistence or lack of match), and هیون = “beast/monster.” The clause therefore says that, by that strength and body, no beast existed (i.e., none could match him).
segments.s240-g340: همه دشت = “the whole plain” (همه premodifier + noun). ازو شد = “became from him / on account of him” (ازو marks cause or source; شد is the past of شدن “to become”). چو introduces a simile “like/as,” دریای خون = “a sea of blood” (دریای is construct/genitive form of دریا). Word order is typical: subject phrase → verbal predicate → simile phrase.
segments.s240-g341: سرش = “his head” (سر + possessive -ش). کردم is the first-person past of کردن “I did / I made”: here it carries the action “I cut” (a common epic narrative usage where the speaker/agent is the hero). از تن = “from the body” (از = from). بخنجر = instrumental phrase “with a dagger” (بـ prefix + خنجر). جدا = adverb “apart / off.” The clause literally: “I made his head apart from the body with a dagger” → “I cut off his head from his body with a dagger.”
segments.s240-g342: چو introduces a simile (“as/like”). باران = “rain.” ازو خون شد = “blood came from him / blood issued from him” (ازو = from him; خون = blood; شد = became/fell—here used to express occurrence). اندر هوا = “into the air” (اندر = in/into; هوا = air). Overall: “blood poured from him like rain into the air.”
segments.s240-g343: ازو ماند = “remained because of it / was left from it” (ازو points back to the preceding event/cause; ماند is past of ماندن “to remain/be left”). کیخسرو is the subject (proper name). اندر شگفت = “in astonishment” (اندر = in; شگفت = wonder/amazement). The phrasing is literary: ‘Kay Khosrow remained in astonishment at it.’
segments.s240-g344: چو = “when.” بنهاد is the past of نهاد/بنهاد “to place/put” (here “he put / set down”). جام = “cup.” آفرین functions as an interjection/acclamation “bravo / well done” (it can also mean ‘praise’ but here it is an exclamation tied to the action). برگرفت is past of برگرفتن “to take up again / lift up.” The clause narrates: “when he put down the cup, he uttered ‘afarin’ and took it up (again).”
segments.s240-g249: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when'. افگنده شد is a passive/perfect construction: افگنده (past participle 'cast/struck') + شد (past copula/auxiliary) = 'was struck/was cast'. شست is the patient/object here (literally 'thumb' but used as the part affected). مرد دلیر is the noun phrase identifying who suffered the action ('the brave man'); word order places the predicate (افگنده شد) before the identifying noun phrase, a common epic/poetic ordering.
segments.s240-g250: بگرز is a verb here meaning to turn/whirl (the mace 'whirled'); اندر آمد is a two‑word phrase 'came in/entered' (اندر = 'into/in', آمد = past 'came'). ز marks source 'from' (poetic form), شمشیر is 'sword' and شیر qualifies it as 'lion' (i.e. 'lion's sword' or 'a sword like a lion'). The whole segment describes the mace whirling in as if coming from a lion‑like sword.
segments.s240-g251: همی is an adverb indicating continuous or iterative action here ('kept on' / 'rained down'). گرز is the subject 'mace/club' and بارید is the past verb 'rained/fell' — together: 'the mace kept raining down'. همچون introduces the simile 'like' and تگرگ is the comparison term 'hail'.
segments.s240-g252: Again همی marks repeated/continuous action. The reduplication چاک چاک is onomatopoeic/repetitive, functioning as a verbal/adverbial phrase 'rending and tearing' (intensified action). آمد is the past 'came' (here 'came ripping'). از خود literally 'from itself' (from its own body/part), and و links to ترگ (shreds/rags) — the clause describes things being torn off or coming away in shreds.
segments.s240-g253: ازیشان is a fused/prepositional phrase 'from/of them' marking the group affected. چهل مرد دیگر is the quantified object 'forty more men' (مرد = men, دیگر = 'other/more'). بکشت is an archaic past form meaning 'he killed' (ب- preverb + کشت, past of کشتن); the verb takes the preceding noun phrase as its object.
segments.s240-g254: غمی here functions as a proper name (or a nominal subject) in this epic context rather than the common noun 'grief'. شد سپهدار is a copular construction: شد = 'became' + سپهدار = 'commander' (title). و بنمود پشت is coordinated: و = 'and', بنمود (پیش‌نهادی/archaic past) = 'showed/revealed' or 'turned' and پشت = 'back' (i.e. 'turned his back' / retreated).
segments.s240-g255: ازو is a prepositional phrase 'on/at him' (from him / toward him). بستد is a past verb meaning 'closed (in on), beset, surrounded' here — an intransitive or transitive past used with ازو to indicate approaching/encircling. آن چار پیل سپید is the noun phrase 'those four white elephants' (آن = 'those/the', چار = 'four', پیل = 'elephants', سپید = 'white'), placed after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s240-g256: شدند is the plural past 'they became'. آن سپاه is the subject 'that army/host'. از جهان ناامید is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the world hopeless' — i.e. 'hopeless of the world' or 'despairing of life'. The clause therefore states that that army became hopeless/despaired.
segments.s240-g345: بران کو is a topical/relative phrase: بران (literally 'upon that'/'for that one') introduces the person under discussion and the enclitic/old relative کو links it to the clause. چنان is an adverb/adjective 'such' modifying پهلوان (the direct object). پهلوان is the noun 'champion/hero'. آفرید is the simple past 'created/made' (third person), finishing the clause. Word order here is topical phrase → object → verb (poetic/older Persian).
segments.s240-g346: کسی ... ندید is a negative indefinite construction: کسی here functions as 'no one' (negative reading) and ندید is the past tense 'did not see'. این شگفتی is the definite object 'this marvel' placed before the adverbial بگیتی ('in the world'), producing an SOV-like order typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s240-g347: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). مردم بود is literally 'was among the people' (مردم before بود). خود is an emphatic pronoun 'himself'. بکردار is a predicate adjective/compound 'of good deeds/virtuous' describing the person. اوی is an archaic third‑person pronoun ('he/him') referencing the same person; here it reiterates the subject.
segments.s240-g348: This clause lists qualities: بمردی is a noun/adverb 'in manliness' or 'manly' (quality), followed by و 'and'. بالا is an adjective 'high/lofty' (= tall). Another و connects to دیدار اوی, where دیدار (here) denotes the person's look/appearance and اوی is the third‑person pronoun 'his/he'. The elements form a coordinate list of characteristics.
segments.s240-g349: همی گفت is a habitual/progressive past: همی marks continuity or habituality and گفت is the past 'said', together giving 'he would say / he kept saying'. اگر is the conditional 'if'. کردگار سپهر is a genitival noun phrase 'the Creator of the sky/heavens' (کردگار = Creator/God; سپهر = the heavens).
segments.s240-g350: ندادی is the past (negative here) 'had not given' / 'did not give' (negation built into the verb). مرا is the object pronoun 'to me' (here written split as م + را in the word list). بهره 'share' is the noun governed by از داد و مهر: از marks the source 'of' and داد 'justice' and مهر 'favour/kindness' are coordinated; overall: 'did not give me a share of justice and favour.' The verb precedes its object in this poetic order.
segments.s240-g351: نبودی is negative past/existential 'there would not be / there was not' (ن + بودی). بگیتی 'in the world' is an adverbial phrase. چنین is 'such/so'. کهترم is an adjective 'more lowly/meaner' with the first‑person suffix -م ('I' / 'myself'), giving 'so lowly/mean as I' — here used as a comparative/predicate.
segments.s240-g352: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. هزمان means 'simultaneously / at the same time'. بدو is the prepositional/benefactive pronoun 'to him/unto it'. دیو و پیل is a coordinated noun phrase 'a demon and an elephant'. اشکرم is formed from اشکر (here 'gratitude') plus the enclitic -م ('my') and so functions as 'my gratitude' (it can also be read as a verb 'I thank' in some dialectal/poetic uses, but here the noun sense fits the construction). The clause thus says that no one so lowly exists for whom, at the same time, a demon and an elephant would be (the objects/sources of) his/my gratitude.
segments.s240-g353: "دو هفته" is a duration phrase ('for two weeks') placed before the verb; "بران گونه" is an adjectival/locative phrase meaning 'in that manner' ("بران" = 'upon/thereon' + "گونه" = 'manner, way'). "بودند" is the past plural copula ('they were') and "شاد" is the predicate adjective ('merry'). The order (duration + locative phrase + copula + predicate) is typical of Persian narrative/poetry.
segments.s240-g354: The preposition "ز" ('from') introduces a source phrase "ز اکوان وز" ('from the heavens' gust/blast'); "اکوان" = 'the heavens/cosmos' and "وز" = 'gust/blast' (poetic). "بزم کردند" is a past plural verb phrase ('they made/held a feast/revel'), and "یاد" here is a noun used adverbially/objectively meaning 'remembrance, in memory of'. Word order places the source phrase up front, then the verb + noun cluster: 'from the heavens' gusts they held revels in remembrance.'
segments.s240-g355: "سه دیگر" = 'the three others' (subject). "تهمتن" is a proper name/epithet in apposition. "چنین" is an adverb ('thus, in this way') and "کرد" is the past verb 'made/did'. "رای" is the object meaning 'plan/counsel' — together: 'the three others, Tahmētan, thus made a plan.' The compact appositional sequence and verb-final clause are typical of epic diction.
segments.s240-g356: "که" introduces the reported content clause ('that...'). "پیروز و شادان" are coordinated predicative adjectives ('victorious and joyful') and "شود" is the 3rd-person subjunctive/optative of شدن used to express 'should/would become' or 'may be'. "باز" ('again') modifies the following noun "جای" ('place/position'), so the clause reads 'that he become victorious and joyful and return to his place.'
segments.s240-g357: This clause reports speech: گفت (said) is the reporting verb at the end, typical of Persian narrative. سامست is a contracted poetic form of سام است (‘Sam is’ or 'it is Sam'), and the sequence بویه زال lists names in apposition; مرا (to me) marks the experiencer/indirect object. را here (written separately) is the object/ topicalizing marker attached earlier in the line; the whole string yields 'Sam said to me...'.
segments.s240-g358: چنین modifies آرزو and means 'such/this sort of'; آرزو را uses را as the definite/object marker. نشاید is an impersonal/modal negative (classical form) meaning 'it is not fitting / one ought not'; نهفت is the (verbal/adjectival) root 'to hide' or 'hidden'. Together نشاید نهفت = 'it should not be hidden.'
