segments.s240-g359: شوم is a subjunctive/jussive form meaning roughly 'I shall / may I be' used here to mark a near-future action ('I will'), and زود is the adverb 'soon.' و simply coordinates. آیم is the present-subjunctive form 'I come' used with future sense, followed by بدرگاه ('to the court / royal presence') and باز ('again'). Word order places the verbal forms before/after phrases in idiomatic classical Persian.
segments.s240-g360: بباید is an emphatic/archaic form of باید meaning 'must/ought'; همی is an emphatic adverb ('indeed/ever/now') strengthening the necessity. کینه را marks the object 'the grudge / vengeance' with را. کرد + ساز is a light-verb + noun combination: کرد ('do/make') + ساز ('device/means, plan') = 'to make (a) plan / prepare (a) device' — here 'prepare/plot revenge.'
segments.s240-g297: چو is the subordinating temporal particle 'when'. ببرید is the past/action verb ('cut off' / 'took') placed before its subject (verb–subject order is common in older Persian poetry); رستم is the agent. سر دیو پست is the direct object: سر 'head' + دیو 'demon' with the epithet پست placed after the noun (postpositive adjective meaning 'base/despicable').
segments.s240-g298: بران is an adverbial/linking word 'thereupon' (literally 'on that'); باره here functions adverbially ('on that occasion' / 'thereupon'). پیل پیکر is a compound-like noun phrase 'elephant-bodied' (پیل modifying پیکر = 'body/figure'), and نشست is the past verb 'sat down' (third person), completing the action begun in the previous clause.
segments.s240-g299: به پیش 'to before / into the presence' is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb; اندر آورد is the compound verbal idea 'brought in' (آورد = 'brought'). یکسر here qualifies the following noun as 'whole/entire' (a whole flock), and گله is the object 'flock/herd'. Word order is prepositional phrase + verb + object.
segments.s240-g300: This clause is elliptic/compact: بنه is a noun ('heap/camp' or 'bundle') placed up front, هرچ کردند is a relative/indefinite clause 'they did whatever', with کردند the past plural 'they did'. ترکان is the subject 'the Turks' placed after the verb (verb–subject inversion), and یله is a predicative adjective 'loose/unrestrained' describing them.
segments.s240-g301: همی رفت is the older/perfective-continuous phrasing meaning 'he kept going' or 'he went on' (همی marks ongoing/repeated action). با پیل و با خواسته are two coordinated instrumental/companial phrases ('with the elephant and with his will/desire'), with the repeated با emphasizing each accompaniment.
segments.s240-g302: وزو is a poetic fused form meaning 'and from that' (و + ز + او / 'and from it'), introducing the result clause. شد is the past 'became', and جهان یکسر آراسته is the predicate 'the world was entirely adorned' (یکسر = 'entirely' prepositive, آراسته = 'adorned').
segments.s240-g303: ز ره 'from the road' is a poetic prepositional phrase (ز = 'from', ره = 'way/road'). چون ... آمد is the temporal clause 'when ... came'; here چون functions as 'when' and آمد is the past 'came'. این آگهی is 'this news' (object/subject depending on syntax) referring to the information that follows.
segments.s240-g304: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. برگشت is the past 'returned' and رستم is the agent (verb–subject order). بدل 'ب' here is the prepositional prefix 'to/into' attached to the demonstrative/particle دان (an archaic/poetic element forming 'بدان' = 'to that'); فرهی is an epithet 'noble/cultured' modifying the referent. Overall the clause means 'that Rostam returned to that noble one', with some archaic fused prepositional forms.
segments.s240-g161: بدست here is a prepositional/ circumstantial marker 'by/with' attached to 'دست' and applies to the following coordinated body parts (چپ و پای). The phrase places the instrument/side ('with his left hand and foot') before the verb. کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/did' and takes آشناه (an adjective/participial predicate meaning 'familiar/ready') as its complement: literally 'he made (them) familiar/ready.' Word order (instrument — verb — result adjective) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s240-g162: بدیگر is an adverbial phrase 'to another (place/direction)'. ز + دشمن marks origin/starting-point 'from the enemy.' همی precedes the past-form جست to give the verbal action its classical narrative/continuous flavour (in Shahnameh همی + past often renders a vivid past action), so همی جست = 'he sprang/ leapt.' راه is the goal/way adverb ('by a way' / 'in a direction'), so the whole segment means 'he sprang away from the enemy in another direction.'
segments.s240-g163: بکارش ('be kār-e-ash') here means 'be of use/fit for him' (idiomatically 'suit him'). نیامد is literal 'did not come' but in this idiom functions as 'was not fitting/was not of use.' زمانی درنگ is a temporal noun phrase 'a pause/ delay for a time' (i.e. even for a moment). Together the clause says that a pause for any time was not fitting for him.
segments.s240-g164: چنین باشد is a formulaic predication 'such is / so it is.' آن کو is a classical relative construction 'that one who' (کو here introduces the relative clause). بود is the past copula 'was.' مرد جنگ is a noun phrase 'man of war / warrior.' The line therefore states a general characterization: 'Such is the man who was a warrior.' Note: کو is an archaic relative particle here, not the locative 'where.'
segments.s240-g165: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). ماندی is a past/hypothetical 'remained' and کس is the indefinite 'anyone/someone' (placed after the verb for emphasis). بمردی is a result clause formed with a b- prefix + past stem to express the hypothetical consequence ('would die' / 'would have died' here). بپای is an adverbial/locative phrase indicating 'at his feet / in his presence / opposing him' (the exact nuance depends on idiom). The clause means 'If anyone stayed, he would die at his feet.'
segments.s240-g166: پی او = 'after him / in pursuit of him' (پی is a prepositional/adverbial 'after/following' with the pronoun او). زمانه here names 'the world/fortune/the times' as an agentive or affected subject. نبردی is a verbal form expressing potential/result (classical subjunctive/conditional nuance; contextually 'would not carry/move'), and ز جای 'from its place' expresses motion/position. Together the clause conveys that even the turning world/time would not move from its place in pursuit of him — i.e. the world itself would not change/move against him.
segments.s240-g167: ولیکن is the adversative conjunction 'but/yet.' چنینست is a fixed copular phrase 'it is thus/so it is.' گردنده is a participle/adjective 'turning/rotating' that modifies دهر 'time/age' (here 'the turning/rotating world or fate'). The nominal phrase is thus 'but such is the revolving time/world.'
segments.s240-g168: گهی ... گاه is a paired adverbial contrast 'sometimes ... at other times.' نوش (here) means a pleasant drink/pleasure/delight rather than the literal 'to drink.' یابند is the present/subjunctive plural 'they obtain/find.' ازو ('from it') refers back to دهر (the world/fate). زهر is 'poison.' The clause says that the turning world sometimes yields delight from it, and at other times poison.
segments.s240-g9: ایا introduces a question (interrogative particle). فلسفه‌دان is the head noun 'philosopher'; بسیار functions adverbially/postpositively to qualify گوی, giving the sense 'speaks much' (i.e. talkative). The three words form a noun phrase 'a philosopher who speaks much.'
segments.s240-g10: بپویم is first-person singular optative/subjunctive (literally 'shall I go/pursue' or 'should I pursue'). براهی is the prepositional phrase 'on/along the way'. که introduces a relative clause 'which/that', and گویی مپوی is the embedded clause: گویی here functions as 'you say' (2nd-person present of گفتن), and مپوی is the negative present/imperative form ('do not go/seek') with the negative prefix م‑. Together: 'pursue a way that you say "do not go".'
segments.s240-g11: ترا here is the 2nd-person clitic + object marker split into ت (your) + را (object/topic marker) marking the experiencer/owner; هرچ means 'whatever'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; چشم سر is a compound 'eye and head' (literally 'on the eye, (on) the head') and بگذرد is third-person singular present 'passes' — so the clause reads 'whatever passes before your eye and head.'
segments.s240-g12: نگنجد is a third-person singular present meaning 'does not fit / cannot be contained'. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here 'indeed / at all' or continuous emphasis). در دلت = 'in your heart' where ت is the 2nd-person suffix on دل. با خرد is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with reason/wisdom'. The clause states that such things do not fit in your heart alongside wisdom.
segments.s240-g13: سخن هرچ = 'however much is spoken' (noun + quantifier). بایست here means 'ought to be / is required' or more loosely 'is such that', and نیست negates the predication: 'is not'. توحید is the abstract term 'tawẖīd' — here best read as 'unity (the One)' rather than merely the outward word; so the clause says that speech, however much, does not amount to (real) unity.
segments.s240-g14: به here introduces the experiential/concerned party 'with / as to / for' (roughly 'regarding Him'). ناگفتن و گفتن is a balanced antithesis 'not-saying and saying'. او is the pronoun 'he/it' (referring to the One/God), and یکیست is the predicate 'is one/the same'. Together: 'With respect to Him, saying and not-saying are one.'
segments.s240-g15: تو‌گر is a conditional 'if you...'. سخته‌ای is a predicate 'you are hard / hard-to-please / stern' (second person). شو is the imperative 'become' or 'be', commanding the addressee to adopt the following role. سخن سخته‌گوی is a noun phrase: 'a harsh/rigorous speaker' (سخته‌گوی = 'harsh-tongued' or 'severe critic'). The whole line gives a concessive instruction: if you are severe, then be a severe speaker.
segments.s240-g16: نیاید is third-person negative 'does not come' / 'will not come'. به بن = 'to the root/foundation' (بن = root). هرگز 'never' intensifies the negation. این گفت و گوی is 'this saying and speaking / this discussion' (گفت و گو 'discussion'); literally: 'This discourse will never come to the root.' Grammatically, the verb precedes the prepositional phrase for emphasis, a common poetic order.
segments.s240-g57: This clause is a short predication with a demonstrative-style opener. یکی (here 'a certain / one') introduces an individual, نره شیرست is a nominal predicate 'is a male-lion / lion-like' (نره = male animal + شیرست = 'lion(-like)'); گویی is a parenthetical particle meaning 'as if / it seemed' (poetic usage) and dژم is a predicative adjective 'grim, sullen'. Word order is somewhat inverted and compressed in the poem: 'one (was) male-lion; it seemed grim.'
segments.s240-g58: همی is an archaic progressive/adverbial particle ('was / kept on'), so همی بفگند gives a continuous action 'were throwing/tossing.' بفگند is a classical/perfective verb form 'to throw / cast,' یال اسپان ز هم is an adverbial/direct object phrase 'the manes of the horses from one another' (ز هم = at one another / from each other). The whole describes a repeated/turbulent action of tossing horse-manes.
segments.s240-g59: بدانست is a past-tense perfect 'he knew / perceived.' The subject name follows the verb (typical poetic inversion): 'خسرو' names the knower. که introduces the subordinate clause; آن نیست گور literally 'that is not a tomb' (آن = that; نیست = is not; گور = tomb/grave).
segments.s240-g60: This is the relative/explanatory clause introduced again by که: برنگذرد is a negative present/subjunctive-form meaning 'will not / does not pass,' so the clause reads 'a grave that will not be crossed.' The sequence 'گور ز اسپی بزور' places the head noun (گور) before its modifiers: 'by force (بزور) from/over a horse (ز اسپی)' — i.e., 'which a horse would not cross by force.' The word order is poetic/inverted but the relations are standard (verb + object + agent/means).
segments.s240-g61: برستم here functions as a prepositional phrase 'بر رستم' (= 'to/against Rostam') written in the compact poetic form; thus the clause reads 'To Rostam, thus he said.' چنین modifies گفت 'thus he said.' کین رنج نیز is a compact noun phrase 'this grief/vengeance/bitterness as well' (کین رنج = a grudging pain or grievance), with نیز 'also' appended.
segments.s240-g62: This is an imperative/instructional clause. به پیگار is an adverbial phrase 'in/for battle' (به + پیگار = 'in the fight'), بر خویشتن is the target 'upon yourself,' and سنج is an imperative 'weigh / measure' (command form). The final نیز again adds 'also / too.' So literally: 'In battle, weigh it upon yourself too.'
segments.s240-g63: This is a pair of imperatives: برو 'go / depart' and نگه‌دار 'keep / guard' (here used as an imperative 'guard yourself'). خویشتن را is the emphasized object 'yourself' marked by را, and ازوی is a prepositional phrase 'from him / away from him.' The line gives an instruction: 'Go and guard yourself from him.'
segments.s240-g64: مگر... باشد introduces an exception/conditional 'unless there be...' (مگر = unless). باشد is the subjunctive/present form 'be / there is,' and the following nominal phrase 'آهرمن کینه‌جوی' names the (hypothetical) person: 'Ahriman, the vengeance-seeker.' The clause means 'unless he is Ahriman the vengeful one.'
segments.s240-g81: چنین گفت is a fixed word order meaning “he said thus/thus he said” (چنین 'thus' before the verb). کین را (کین + object marker را) marks ‘this vengeance’ as the direct object. نباید فگند is an impersonal/old-fashioned negative modal: نباید (‘one must not / it is not proper to’) plus فگند (classical/poetic past-stem of انداختن ‘to throw’), literally ‘one must not cast (it) away’ — the object (کین را) belongs to this verb.
segments.s240-g82: بباید here is the positive counterpart of نباید, an impersonal modal ‘one must/ought’. گرفتن is the infinitival/masdar-like verbal noun ‘to take/capture’ that completes بباید. بخم کمند is a poetic noun phrase: کمند ‘lasso’ modified by بخم (an older/obscure adjective meaning something like ‘coiled/looped’ or a particular part of the snare); the whole phrase is the object or instrument of گرفتن: ‘to seize by the coiled loop of the lasso.’ Word order places the modal first, then the verbal noun, then the instrument phrase.
segments.s240-g83: نشایدش is a fused impersonal/personal expression meaning ‘it is not fitting/proper (for him/it)’, with ـش clitic referring to the person. کردن is the light verb/infinitive ‘to do’ that forms a verbal phrase with the following instrument. بخنجر is an instrumental prepositional phrase (با + خنجر contracted as بخنجر) meaning ‘with a dagger’. تباه here is an adjectival/verb-like word ‘ruin/kill’ that with کردن gives the sense ‘to put to death / destroy’ — so the clause means ‘it is not fitting to destroy him with a dagger.’
segments.s240-g84: بدین سانش is a formulaic phrase ‘by this manner/thus’ (بدین = به + دین/this, سانش = سان + ـش ‘its way’), where the whole adverbial phrase modifies the following verb. زنده is an adjective/adverb ‘alive’ describing the object to be brought. برم is the present-subjunctive/optative form of بردن ‘to carry/bring’ in the first person singular (here narrative/future sense ‘I will bring’). نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase ‘before/in the presence of the king’; word order is adverbial (بدین سانش زنده) + verb (برم) + goal (نزد شاه).
segments.s240-g85: بینداخت is the past tense / perfective of انداختن with the prefix بی‑ (poetic variant) meaning ‘threw/cast’. The usual subject (رستم کیانی) follows the verb here (verb‑subject order is common in classical Persian). کمند is the direct object ‘the lasso’ (کمند follows the name). The sequence is V – subject – modifier – object: ‘(He) cast, Rostam the Kayanian, the lasso.’
segments.s240-g86: همی + past verb (همی خواست) is a classical imperfective/ingressive construction meaning ‘was about to / tried to / intended to’. کارد سرش را ببند is an object‑clause ofخواست: کارد ‘knife/dagger’ + سرش را (‘his head’ with possessive clitic and object marker) + ببند, the (subjunctive/imperative-form) base of بستن ‘to bind/close’. Together they express his intention: ‘he intended to fasten/press the knife to his head’ (literal), i.e. to use the dagger on his head. Note the classical placement of the object marker را after the possessed noun (سرش را).
segments.s240-g87: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction ‘when’. گور دلاور is a noun phrase with گور (either a name or an animal-noun) modified by دلاور ‘valiant/brave’; it is the subject. کمندش is the lasso with the possessive clitic ـش ‘his’ (کمند + ـش) and is the object of بدید. بدید is the past tense of دیدن ‘to see’. Word order is ‘When X saw his lasso…’.
segments.s240-g88: شد is the past/perfective auxiliary ‘became/happened’ used here to introduce the result clause. از چشم او is an idiom literally ‘from his eye’ meaning ‘from his sight’ (از + چشم + pronoun او). در زمان ناپدید places the event in time: در زمان ‘in the moment/time’ + ناپدید ‘vanished/unseen’ — altogether ‘it became (i.e. he) vanished from his sight in an instant.’ Note the classical concatenation of prepositional and temporal phrases after the verb.
segments.s240-g49: که here functions as a subordinating/relative conjunction ('that'/'when') introducing the clause. گوری is the subject noun (here a color/type name for an animal — sense not listed in the provided options). پدید آمد is a two-word verbal phrase: پدید (an adverb/verb-stem 'appear') precedes آمد (past 3rd sg. 'came/appeared') so together mean 'appeared'. اندر گله is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/among the flock'), with اندر = 'in/within' following the verb, which is common in older poetic word order.
segments.s240-g50: چو is the comparative/simile marker 'as'/'like' starting a simile. شیری is used as a noun/adjective meaning 'lion-like' or 'a lion' (leonine). که introduces a relative clause modifying شیری ('that/which'). از بند گردد یله is the relative clause: از بند = 'from the bond/fetter', گردد (3rd sg. of گشت/گردیدن) functions as 'becomes/goes' in the sense 'breaks free' or 'is set free', and یله is a predicate adjective 'loose, unrestrained'. The clause literally reads 'that from its bond becomes loose.'
segments.s240-g51: همان is a demonstrative/intensifier 'the same'/'exactly that' modifying رنگ ('color'). رنگ خورشید is a genitive construction 'color of the sun' (noun + possessor). دارد is the present 3rd person auxiliary/main verb 'has' that follows the possessed phrase here. درست is an adverb/postpositive intensifier meaning 'exactly' or 'truly', emphasizing the exactness of the resemblance.
segments.s240-g52: سپهرش is سپهر ('sky, heaven') with the enclitic possessive -ش 'its/their', so 'its heavens'. بزر (archaic form of بزرگ) is an adjective 'great' placed before آب 'water' to form the compound idea 'great water' or 'mighty waters'. گویی here is a parenthetical particle meaning 'as if'/'one might say' (sense not in the provided list). بشست is the past 3rd sg. of شستن 'washed' — together 'گویی بشست' produces the simile 'as if (they) had been washed by great waters.' The verb is past-tense narrative describing the imagined action.
segments.s240-g53: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one'/'a single'. برکشیده is a past participial/adjectival form 'raised/drawn up' placed before its noun, functioning like 'drawn'. خط is the noun 'line/stripe' that the participle modifies. از یال اوی is the prepositional phrase 'from his mane' (از = 'from', یال = 'mane', اوی = possessive 'his'), with the possessive clitic following the noun, as is usual in classical Persian.
segments.s240-g54: ز is the poetic form of از ('from') introducing the starting point. مشک سیه is a noun + adjective 'musk-black' or 'black like musk' (مشک = 'musk', سیه = 'black/dark'), with adjective following the noun. تا marks extent 'as far as'/'down to' and is followed by بدنبال اوی: بدنبال ('behind/at the rear of' or 'in pursuit of') plus the possessive اوی 'his', yielding 'as far as behind him' — together expressing the stripe's extent from mane to his hind/rear.
segments.s240-g55: سمندی is a noun (سمند) 'steed, warhorse' with the -ی ending forming an adjectival/noun form referring to the horse. بزرگست is an archaic copular/adjectival phrase 'is great' (بزرگ + -ست 'is'). گویی is the parenthetical particle 'as if'/'one would say' (not listed in the given senses). بجای here means 'in place of'/'instead of' and links to the following noun phrase that specifies what is being replaced; the sequence 'گویی بجای' produces the simile 'as if, in place of ...'.
segments.s240-g56: ورا is the dative/indirect-object form 'to it' or 'for it' (ورا = و + را/archaic form meaning 'to/for him/it'). چار is the numeral 'four'. گرزست is a nominal-predicative construction 'is (a) mace' or 'are like maces' (گرز = 'mace/club' + -ست 'is'), so چار گرزست = 'four (are) maces'. آن دست و پای is a nominative/contrastive phrase 'those hands and feet' (آن = 'those/the', دست = 'hand', و = 'and', پای = 'foot'); together the line means 'for it, four maces are those hands and feet' — idiomatically, 'its four limbs are like maces.'
segments.s240-g153: This is reported speech introduced by the verb گفت (said). The quoted clause is بجایی بخواهم فگندنت: بخواهم is 1st-person present/subjunctive ('I want/I will'), فگند- is the archaic verb فگندن 'to throw/cast' with the 2nd-person object suffix -ت ('you'), and بجایی is an adverbial phrase 'to a fitting/place' placed before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s240-g154: که here introduces the content clause (that ...). اندر دو گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'in the two worlds' (i.e. both realms). بمانی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you remain' (subordinate optative after گفت), and نهفت is an adjective/participial form 'hidden/concealed' describing the intended state.
segments.s240-g155: بدریای ژرف functions as the goal phrase 'into the deep sea' (بیـدریا/بـه دریا in archaic spelling), where ژرف is the adjective 'deep'. اندر انداختش is a compound past verb: اندر + انداخت (he threw) with enclitic -ش 'her/it' marking the object.
segments.s240-g156: ز introduces cause/reason 'because of' or 'from'. کینه خور is a compound meaning 'hate-eating'—i.e. 'full of hatred' or 'hating/vengeful' with خور used as present-stem 'eating' modifying ماهیان 'the fishes'. ساختش is a past transitive verb with enclitic -ش 'them/her/it' meaning 'they did to her' or 'they brought it about' (here: treated/destroyed her).
segments.s240-g157: همان ... کز ... رسید is a relative clause: همان 'that same (one)', کز (archaic contraction of که از) 'who/that from', هوا 'the air', سوی دریا 'toward the sea', and رسید is the simple past 'arrived' (i.e. 'who came/arrived').
segments.s240-g158: This clause contains the action: سبک تیغ تیز (a light/quick, sharp sword) is the object phrase; از میان is a source phrase 'from between/from the middle', and برکشید is the past stem meaning here 'drew (a weapon)'—the verb precedes or follows its object in flexible epic word order.
segments.s240-g159: نهنگان 'the whales' is the subject. که introduces a relative clause 'that', کردند is the past plural 'they made/did', and آهنگ اوی means 'their intention/aim toward him'—آهنگ in this sense is 'aim/movement' and اوی is the 3rd-person pronoun 'him/his'.
segments.s240-g160: ببودند is an older/perfective past plural 'they were'; سرگشته is a predicate adjective 'bewildered/dazed'. از چنگ اوی literally 'from his claw' uses از to mark cause/agent and چنگ 'claw' (here 'his claws' or 'his grasp'), with اوی again the 3rd-person pronoun 'his'.
segments.s240-g369: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the first item; گستردنیها is a plural noun (گستردنی + ها) meaning things for spreading (e.g. carpets/coverings). The clause then repeats the preposition: و (and) + از تخت عاج ('from the ivory throne'), where از + noun marks source; the word order places the preposition before the noun phrase as in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s240-g370: ز again functions as 'from' and starts a coordinated list: دیبا (brocade) و دینار (dinars/money) و پیروزه تاج (literally 'turquoise crown', with پیروزه qualifying تاج). The conjunction و links the nouns; پیروزه is an adjective/stone name placed before تاج in the normal Persian adjective+noun order.
segments.s240-g371: بنزدیک is an adverbial/locative 'nearby'; رستم names the (intended) recipient. فرستاد is the past verb 'sent'; شاه (the subject 'the king') follows the verb, a common poetic word order (V ... S). In older Persian the recipient can appear without a introducing preposition in such constructions.
segments.s240-g372: که introduces a reported clause ('that' / 'saying that'). این هدیه is the demonstrative + noun 'this gift' (direct object of the reported content). با خویشتن is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with himself' (i.e. to go accompanied by it), and بر براه is the archaic prepositional phrase 'on the road' / 'en route' (بر + براه).
segments.s240-g373: یک امروز 'one day / for a day' is an adverbial time phrase placed before the clause. با ما 'with us' is the accompanying phrase. بباید is the modal 'must/ought to'. بدن here appears in a non-standard/archaic form and cannot be matched to the provided noun senses (it functions syntactically as a verbal or copular element 'to be / remain' in context), so its lexical sense is not one of the listed noun senses.
segments.s240-g374: وزان 'and/from that' links to the previous clause; پس 'then/afterwards' is temporal/continuative. تــ (written detached here as ت) + را forms ترا (to you / for you) marking the person concerned. رای رفتن زدن is an idiomatic sequence: رای ('plan/counsel') + رفتن ('to go') + زدن ('to strike/put') — together meaning 'to decide on going' (رای زدن = to decide). Grammatically, رای is the notion (plan) and زدن is the light verb forming the action 'to decide'; the precise lexical sense for زدن here is idiomatic and does not exactly match the provided senses.
segments.s240-g375: ببود is an archaic past copula/auxiliary 'he was' (or 'there was'); و links a second past verb بپیمود (here functioning as a past action 'he spent/traversed/consumed'). چندی is an adverb meaning 'for a while' and modifies the verb that follows; نبید is the object 'wine' (so the clause describes spending a while with wine).
segments.s240-g376: بشبگیر is an adverb/verb meaning 'he stayed until night' or 'overnight'. جز introduces an exception 'except'; رای رفتن (rāy + raftan) again 'the counsel/plan of going', and ندید is the past negative 'he did not see/encounter' — here 'he saw no counsel except to go' (i.e. the only plan counseled was to depart).
segments.s240-g273: بزد is a 3rd-person past singular verb 'he struck/ hit'. The prepositional phrase بر سر دیو (literally 'on the head of the demon') follows the verb in typical Persian word order. چون پیل مست is a simile 'like a drunken elephant' where چون ('like') introduces the comparison; note the poetic omission of an ezāfe between پیل and مست (expected پیلِ مست).
segments.s240-g274: سر و مغزش is a coordinated noun phrase 'his head and his brain'; only مغزش bears the possessive suffix ـش, but in Persian such a suffix can be understood to apply to the whole coordinated phrase. از گرز او گشت پست is a passive/ resultative expression: از (causal/instrumental) + گرز او ('his mace') indicates the instrument or cause, گشت (past of گشتن) = 'became/was made', and پست = 'low/ humbled'.
segments.s240-g275: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'fell/ descended' (فرود + آمد). آن is the demonstrative 'that' and آبگون is an adjective 'bluish/ water-colored' placed before the noun in poetic word order. خنجرش 'his dagger' uses the possessive suffix ـش attached to خنجر.
segments.s240-g276: برآهیخت is a past 3rd-person form 'he/it sprang up, rose up'. و is the coordinating 'and'. ببرید here functions as a past action 'cut off / took' (poetic past form of بریدن with the b- prefix). جنگی modifies سر (an adjective 'warlike' or 'of war'); سرش 'his head' shows the possessive suffix ـش. The clause therefore reports a sudden motion followed by severing the (warlike) head.
segments.s240-g277: همی خواند is a habitual/continuous past construction: همی indicates repeated or continuing action and خواند is the past 'he called/sang/cried out' (here 'he cried out'). بر کردگار is the prepositional phrase 'to/toward the Creator (God)', and آفرین is an interjection/acclamation 'praise' or 'bravo'.
segments.s240-g278: کزو is a fused prepositional/relative 'from whom / from which', introducing the source. بود is the past of 'to be' used here with a sense close to 'came/was from', and پیروزی و زور کین lists the results: 'victory and the force/strength of vengeance' (زور modified by کین). Note the poetic omission of ezāfe between زور and کین.
segments.s240-g279: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; مر is an enclitic (here 'to me' / 'for me') used idiomatically to add the speaker's perspective or emphasis. دیو را marks 'the demon' with the definite object marker را. مردم بد شناس is an appositive/predicate 'people, bad-knowers'—here meaning 'you people judge (or know) [the demon] wrongly' (بدشناس = 'one who knows poorly' formed from شناس 'knower' + بد 'bad').
segments.s240-g280: کسی کو ندارد is an idiomatic negative existential: کسی 'someone/anyone', کو is an archaic relative 'who/that', ندارد 'does not have' (present/past sense used poetically). ز یزدان سپاس is 'thanksgiving/praise toward God' with ز as the preposition 'from/for/toward' and سپاس 'thanks' as the noun. The clause asserts there is no one lacking gratitude to God.
segments.s240-g121: The clause places the noun 'زمین' (earth/ground) before the verb for emphasis; 'گرد' here works adverbially as 'around'/'round' modifying the action. 'ببرید' is a past/poetic verbal form meaning 'tore up / carried off' (here functioning as the main past action). 'برداشتش' is the verb برداشت with the enclitic object pronoun -ش ('it'), meaning 'took/lifted it' and links the object (the earth/its produce) forward to the next clause.
segments.s240-g122: 'ز' is the preposition 'from'; 'هامون' names the plain as the origin. 'بگردون' is a verbal form with the poetic/preverbal b- meaning 'turned' or 'caused to turn'; 'برافراشتش' is برافراشت + -ش, 'raised it/upheld it', with the -ش referring back to the thing taken in the previous segment. The sequence shows the motion (from the plain → turning → raising).
segments.s240-g123: 'غمی شد' is a copular construction: the noun 'غمی' (grief) + past of شدن (شد) = 'became sorrow/was filled with grief.' The proper name 'تهمتن' follows (subject placed after the predicate for poetic emphasis): 'Tahmatan became sorrowful.' 'چو' is the temporal 'when', 'بیدار' is the adjective 'awake', and the final 'شد' is the past auxiliary 'became/was' giving 'when he awoke.'
segments.s240-g124: This compact line stacks nominal phrases: 'سر پر خرد' = 'a head full of wisdom' (سر 'head' + پر 'full (of)' + خرد 'wisdom'). Then 'پر ز پیکار شد' repeats the 'پر' pattern with 'ز' ('from/because of') + 'پیکار' ('battle') + 'شد' ('became'): literally 'became full, from battle' — idiomatically 'became full of warlike purpose/counsel.' Note the repeated 'پر' emphasises fullness of qualities.
segments.s240-g125: 'چو' = 'when'; 'رستم بجنبید' is past 'Rostam moved/stirred' (بجنبید here is a past form meaning 'was roused / stirred'). 'بر خویشتن' is a reflexive/prepositional phrase 'upon himself' (بر + خویشتن), literally 'he roused himself' or 'he stirred his own body.'
segments.s240-g126: 'بدو' = 'to him' (dative/prepositional 'unto him'); 'گفت' is the past 'said' (verb). The subject 'اکوان' follows the verb for poetic inversion: 'Akvan said to him.' The phrase 'که‌ای پیلتن' functions as the quoted address: 'که‌ای' here acts like an interrogative/vocative 'O / who are you' (poetic combination of که + ای), and 'پیلتن' is the vocative name 'Piltan.'
segments.s240-g127: 'یکی' here means 'someone' (an indefinite person); 'آرزو کن' is an imperative 'make a wish/try/call (someone)' — in context it functions as 'send someone to try' or 'have someone attempt.' The conjunctional pair 'که تا' gives purpose/indirect question sense ('so that / in order that' / 'to find out'), and 'از هوا' is the prepositional phrase 'from the air / from the wind' indicating origin/direction.
segments.s240-g128: 'کجات' = 'from where / which quarter' (interrogative adverb). 'آید' is the verb 'comes' (present/past sense here: 'comes/is coming'). 'افگندن' is the infinitive 'to throw / to cast' and is the action attributed to the wind; 'اکنون' = 'now' modifies the temporal frame; 'هوا' = 'the air / the wind' is the agent. The word order is compact/poetic: 'from where it comes (آید), to throw — now the wind' = 'from which quarter the wind now comes to fling.'
segments.s240-g201: سواران (noun) is the subject 'the horsemen'. که بودند با او is a relative/subordinate clause 'who were with him' (که introduces the clause; بودند is the past tense 'were'). بخواند is an archaic/poetic third-person past/plural shout-form (classical Persian often uses -د where New Persian would have -دند), here meaning 'they cried out/called aloud' and links to the subject سواران.
segments.s240-g202: بر اسپ سرافرازشان برنشاند: بر + اسپ = 'on/onto the horse(s)'; سرافرازشان is a possessive adjective 'their proud/lofty (horses)'; برنشاند is a causative/past action 'they set/placed (themselves) upon' (here idiomatically 'they mounted'). The verb is in the classical past form and agrees with the plural subject (elliptically expressed in poetry).
segments.s240-g203: گرفتند is the past tense 'they seized/took' (third-person plural). هر کس is an indefinite distributive 'each one' (هر = each, کس = person). کمند و کمان are the direct objects 'a lariat/lasso and a bow', joined by و 'and'. The phrase describes each rider arming himself.
segments.s240-g204: بدان تا که باشد چنین بدگمان is a purpose/result clause. بد (prepositional prefix) + دان functions idiomatically as 'by that / with that' (i.e., 'thereby'); تا که introduces the purpose/subordinate clause 'so that' and باشد is the classical present/subjunctive 'may be / might be'. چنین بدگمان = 'thus suspicious' (بدگمان = suspicious/uneasy). Together: 'with that so that he may be thus suspicious.'
segments.s240-g205: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'when'). یارد here means 'a companion/helper' (a person arriving). بدین is the prepositional 'to/into this' (ب- + دین), and مرغزار is 'meadow'; آمدن is the infinitive 'to come' used in classical epic style where the bare infinitive stands for a past action (Ferdowsi often uses آمدن where New Persian uses آمد/آمدند), so the clause means 'that a companion came into this meadow.'
segments.s240-g206: بنزدیک is an adverb/adpositional phrase 'near, close at hand'. چندین is a quantifier 'several'. سوار (here) 'horsemen/riders'. آمدن again is the infinitive used in narrative to indicate the past action 'came' (classical poetic usage). The segment reads 'several riders came near.'
segments.s240-g207: پس 'then' (sequence connector). اندر سواران = 'into/among the horsemen' (اندر = in/among). برفتند is a compound past 'they rode/advanced' (بر + رفتند; classical past form). گرم here modifies the action 'eagerly/fiercely' (گرم = zealous/fervent). The whole phrase means 'then they rode in among the horsemen eagerly.'
segments.s240-g208: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/that'. بر + پشت = 'upon the back (of)'; رستم is the proper name Rostam (object of بر پشت). بدرند is the classical form meaning 'they tear' (here in a purpose sense 'that they might tear' — classical subjunctive/purpose usage). چرم is the object 'leather' (i.e., hide, leather garment or saddle leather). Altogether: 'so that they might tear the leather on Rostam's back.'
segments.s240-g329: بمی (an adjectival/place marker) precedes the name to indicate 'of Bam' (a place) and is attached before the proper name; رستم is the name. آن داستان برگشاد is a compact past-tense clause: آن 'that' + داستان 'tale/story' + برگشاد (past of برگشودن) 'opened/unfolded'. Word order is typical Persian SOV compressed for poetry: subject ('بمی رستم') then object ('آن داستان') then verb ('برگشاد').
segments.s240-g330: وز here is the poetic contraction of و + ز (and from / and by); اکوان 'the ages' or 'worlds' is the oblique noun. همی + کرد places the verb in an iterative or habitual past (common in epic Persian) so 'همی کرد' = 'he would/kept doing/made'. بر شاه یاد is an old construction meaning 'to (the) king a reminder' — کرد governs an object expressed by یاد ('reminder') with بر + indirect object 'شاه'.
segments.s240-g331: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that / which'. گوری 'a grave/tomb' is the topic, ندیدم is simple past first-person negative 'I did not see / I have not seen', and بخوبی چنوی is a compressed comparative phrase: بخوبی 'so well/as well' + چنوی ('چنو' = 'such / as') producing 'as well/such as that'. Word order reflects poetic inversion: comparative phrase follows the verb.
segments.s240-g332: بدان is the fossilized form of به + آن meaning 'in/with that' or 'by that'; سرافرازی 'splendor/honor' is the quality being referred to. The list 'و آن رنگ و بوی' continues the enumeration ('and that color and scent'). The sequence names qualities of the grave; note the archaic/poetic use of 'بدان' instead of modern 'به آن'.
segments.s240-g333: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. خنجر 'dagger' is the agent, بدرید is past 'tore/was torn', and بر تنش پوست literally 'on his body skin' — the enclitic ش = 'his' attaches to تن. The clause order is temporal clause (چو ...) followed by the action.
segments.s240-g334: بروبر is an adverb meaning 'directly/face-to-face' or 'altogether'; نبخشود is past negative 'he did not forgive/spare'. The phrase دشمن نه دوست uses نه as 'nor' to give the correlative 'neither enemy nor friend'. The verb governs the following nouns as the objects he did not spare.
segments.s240-g335: سرش uses the enclitic ش 'his' attached to سر 'head'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', so 'سرش چون سر پیل' = 'his head like an elephant's head'. و مویش دراز joins with 'and his hair (مویش) [was] long (دراز)'; the copula is omitted as typical in Persian poetic style.
segments.s240-g336: دهن 'mouth' is the head noun, followed by پر used adjectivally 'full (of)'. زدندانهای is a genitive/compound indicating 'of the teeth' (here 'from the teeth of'), and گراز 'boar' is the possessor; the sequence means 'mouth full of the boar's teeth/tusks.' This is compact nominal modification typical of epic diction.
segments.s240-g281: هرانکو is the fused relative expression هر آن‌که ("whoever"). گذشت is the past/intransitive verb 'passed/left' (3rd sing.), and از ره مردمی is a prepositional phrase (از + ره) with مردمی ("of the people") post‑modifying ره in poetic order — together: "whoever has passed from the people's way."
segments.s240-g282: ز here is the poetic/prepositional ز (= از) marking origin/association: "of/from the ...". دیوان is intended as the group 'demons' in context (poetic plural), followed by the proper name شمر with the epithet مشمر (literally 'armed/girded') — a poetic apposition. از آدمی is a comparative use of از meaning 'rather than / than a': the whole clause says such a person is of the demons (Shamr the armed) rather than of mankind.
segments.s240-g283: خرد‌گر is an agent noun 'the wise person / wise man.' برین is the demonstrative construction (بر + ین) equivalent to 'these/this' in poetic diction, modifying گفتها (plural 'sayings/utterances'). نگرود is the verb (from گرویدن) with negative prefix ن- here meaning 'does not adopt / will not embrace.' Grammatically: 'The wise man does not adopt these sayings.'
segments.s240-g284: مگر is the subordinating particle 'unless / except that.' نیک modifies مغزش ('his good mind' — مغز with possessive suffix -ش). همی is the older adverb often used to mark ongoing or emphatic aspect ('indeed / continually / will'), and نشنود here functions as a present/subjunctive verbal form of شنود 'hear' (classical usage), so the clause means roughly 'unless his good mind indeed hears (them).' Note: the classical verb form and the particle همی are archaic/poetic.
segments.s240-g285: گر is the conditional particle 'if.' آن پهلوانی uses آن + پهلوانی to point to 'that hero/heroic one' (پهلوانی here used as the individual hero/heroic person). بود is the copula 'was/were' (classical narrative), and زورمند is an adjective 'powerful/mighty.' Grammatically this is a protasis: 'If that hero were mighty.'
segments.s240-g286: به functions here instrumentally ('in/with' or 'by'), so به بازو ستبر = 'with/possessing a stout arm.' The adjective ستبر follows the noun (poetic word order). و connects the second coordinate, به بالا بلند = 'and lofty in height' (بالا + بلند). The repeated به + noun + adjective pattern is a common poetic way to list physical attributes.
segments.s240-g287: This is a short, compact poetic clause: گوان خوان و اکوان names or labels are placed in apposition with خوان (here functioning like 'call/name'), and دیوش مـخوان is a negative imperative construction (مخوان = 'do not call/recite') addressing the act of naming him a devil. In plain terms the speaker says 'name him Gavan and Akvan, but do not call him a demon.' The exact historical/mythical names (گوان، اکوان) are used poetically; مخوان is the prohibitive/negative imperative form.
segments.s240-g288: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for / because / that.' بر پهلوانی is a prepositional phrase 'upon heroism / to the hero,' with بر as 'upon/against.' بگردد is the (subjunctive/optative) form of گردیدن 'to turn / to become' meaning 'would turn into / cause,' and زیان is the noun 'harm / detriment.' Altogether: 'for that would bring harm upon heroism.'
segments.s240-g193: بمالیدش is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb (بمالید) with the enclitic object -ش = 'it', meaning 'he rubbed/wiped it'. از گرد is a prepositional phrase 'from/of dust' (گرد here = dust). و links two coordinated past actions; زین (saddle) is the direct object of برنهاد (past of نهاد with the prefix بر-), so the clause reads 'he wiped it of dust and placed the saddle on (the horse)'.
segments.s240-g194: This clause centers on یاد + کرد, literally 'made/ did remembrance' = 'he remembered'. ز یزدان نیکی دهش strings nominal elements before the verb: ز (from/of) + یزدان (God) + نیکی دهش ('a good gift' or 'goodness, a grant'), so the whole clause means 'he remembered the good gift from God' (word order is nominal phrase then verb).
segments.s240-g195: لگامش is لگام (bridle/reins) with the enclitic -ش = 'his/it'. The phrase 'بسر بر زد' functions as a past action 'he struck (it) at/on the head' (بـسر here is an older/poetic locative 'به سر' = 'on the head'); زد is the past of زدن 'to strike'. و connects this to برنشست (past 'he sat upon/mounted'). Overall: 'he struck the bridle on the head and mounted.'
segments.s240-g196: بران is an adverb/locative 'thereupon' or 'on that (thing)'. تیز شمشیر is a noun phrase 'a sharp sword'. بنهاد here is a past verb formed from نهاد (put/placed/laid), so 'بنهاد دست' = 'he put (his) hand' or 'laid his hand (to/toward)'; دست is 'hand'. The clause reads 'thereupon he laid his hand on a sharp sword.'
segments.s240-g197: گله (herd) is the direct object/topic; هر کجا دید is a relative/conditional phrase 'wherever he saw (it)'. یکسر is an adverb 'at once/entirely' modifying براند (past-subjunctive/optative-form in classical usage but here narrative present/past sense) meaning 'he drove away'. The clause: 'Wherever he saw a herd, he drove it off at once.'
segments.s240-g198: بشمشیر is an instrumental prepositional form 'with a sword'. بر نام یزدان is the prepositional phrase 'upon/against the name of God' (here idiomatically 'in the name of God' or 'calling God's name'). بخواند is a 3rd-person singular verbal form from خواندن used here as 'he called/cried out' (i.e. invoked). So: 'with a sword he called on the name of God.'
segments.s240-g199: چون here introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'. گله‌دار is the subject 'the herdsman'. بانگ اسبان is the object/genitive phrase 'the cry of the horses'. شنید is the past-tense verb 'he heard'. Together: 'When the herdsman heard the cry of the horses...'.
segments.s240-g200: سرآسیمه is an adjective/adverb meaning 'distraught/aghast/in a hurry'. از خواب is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from sleep'. سر بر کشید is a fixed verbal expression (سر را بر کشیدن) meaning 'to raise one's head' or idiomatically 'to spring up' — here in past tense 'he sprang up'. The clause: 'distraught, he sprang up from sleep.'
segments.s240-g265: برستی is a past-tense, 2nd/3rd-singular poetic form meaning 'rose/was removed/left'; here it functions as 'he rose/issued forth'. ز + دریا is a prepositional phrase 'from the sea' (ز = 'from'). The coordinated phrase و چنگ نهنگ ('and the whale's claw') is a noun phrase in apposition or simile: چنگ (claw) + نهنگ (whale) form the image 'the whale's claw'.
segments.s240-g266: بدشت آمدی is a simple past clause 'he came to the plain' (بدشت = 'to/on the plain', آمدی = 'came'). باز پیچان is an adverbial participial phrase: باز 'again' + پیچان 'twisting/coilingly' describing the manner of arrival. بجنگ is an infinitive/verbal noun 'to fight' that expresses purpose or manner — 'twisting to fight' (i.e., he came again in a twisting way for battle).
segments.s240-g267: تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (subject). چو بنشید is a temporal subordinate clause 'when he sat down' (چو = 'when'; بنشید = past 'sat down'). گفتار دیو is a noun phrase 'the speech/words of the demon' that serves as the direct object or theme picked up by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s240-g268: برآورد is a past-tense verb 'brought forth/raised' (here 'uttered' or 'gave forth'); its object is the preceding گفتار دیو. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by شیر جنگی غریو 'a war-lion's roar' (شیر = lion, جنگی = warlike/fighter as adjectival modifier, غریو = roar). The clause means he produced a roar like a battle-lion's.
segments.s240-g269: ز فتراک is a prepositional phrase 'from the saddle-cloth / from his cloak' (ز = 'from', فتراک = 'saddle-cloth/cloth'). بگشاد is here verbal/adjectival 'unfolded / took out' (literally 'opened'), modifying the following noun پیچان کمند 'a coiling/spiralling lasso' (پیچان = 'coiling/twisting', کمند = 'lasso/snare').
segments.s240-g270: بیفگند is a past verb 'he cast/threw' (transitive). و simply links the actions. آمد here is intransitive 'came' (past). میانش is میان + -ش 'into/among him/it' (i.e., 'around him' or 'into his midst'). به بند is a locative/result phrase 'into bonds/fetter' (به = 'into/toward', بند = 'bond/fetter'), describing the result of the thrown lasso.
segments.s240-g271: بپیچید is a past-tense verb 'he twisted/turned' (the prefix ب- marks the verb form). بر زین is a locative phrase 'on the saddle' (بر = 'on/upon', زین = 'saddle'). و links the clause to گرز گران 'the heavy mace' (گرز = 'mace/club', گران = 'heavy'), the object/instrument that follows the next verb.
segments.s240-g272: برآهیخت is a past verb meaning 'he raised / was roused up' (here transitive: 'he lifted [the mace]'). چون introduces a simile 'like', followed by پتک آهنگران 'the blacksmiths' sledgehammer' (پتک = sledgehammer, آهنگران = blacksmiths), so the motion is compared to the hammer-blow of smiths.
segments.s240-g217: بجایی (به + جایی) here functions as a locative prepositional phrase 'to the place/at the place' (the fused poetic form بجایی). که introduces a relative clause 'where/that'. هر سال is an adverbial time phrase 'every year'. چوپان گله is a noun phrase with head چوپان 'the shepherd' followed by گله 'flock' (object/possessed item) in the order typical for Persian (possessor/head or head + direct object depending on emphasis).
segments.s240-g218: بران دشت is a prepositional/locative phrase: بران (poetic form) = 'to/onto/at that' and دشت = 'plain'. و simply coordinates the next phrase. آن آب کردی یله is a noun phrase describing a particular stream: آن 'that', آب 'water/stream', کردی functions here as an adjectival/attributive form meaning 'Kurdish' (poetic/old usage), and یله 'loose/wild' is a postpositive adjective describing the stream. The clause as a whole names the place and one of its notable features.
segments.s240-g219: خود here is emphatic 'he himself' (emphatic/reflective pronoun). و simply coordinates. از introduces a partitive/collective expression 'from/among' (from the group of). یل نامدار is a noun phrase 'renowned heroes' (یل = hero, نامدار = famed) with the adjective following the noun as usual in older Persian.
segments.s240-g220: رسیدند is the 3rd-person plural past-perfect/simple past 'they arrived/reached'. تازان here qualifies the group as 'raiders' (an appositive or epithet). بران مرغزار is the locative phrase 'to/at that meadow' (بران = 'to/at that', مرغزار = 'meadow/pasture'). Word order: subject (خود و دو هزار ...) comes before the verb رسیدند, then the locative complement.
segments.s240-g221: This is a coordinated instrumental/elemental phrase listing the means: ابا here is the (poetic) verb/noun 'abā' meaning 'to refuse/be reluctant' or sometimes used to mark an action; in context it introduces what they did/used. باده 'wine', و 'and', رود here is best read as 'string instrument/music' (poetic meaning), و connects again, گردان 'troop' (a body of horsemen or band). بهم (بهم) means 'together' or 'in concert' and modifies the whole list. The sequence gives the means by which they acted.
segments.s240-g222: This clause gives purpose/result. ب here is the prepositional prefix 'by/with'. دان in the manuscript appears as part of the fixed phrase 'بدان' = 'by that/thereby' (here treated as a fused unit; the lemma senses listed do not directly include this demonstrative use). تا is the purposive conjunction 'so that/in order that'. کند is the 3rd-person singular subjunctive/jussive 'may do/should make' (used for purpose). بر دل اندیشه کم is a prepositional phrase: بر = 'on/upon', دل = 'heart', اندیشه = 'thought/worry', کم = 'little/less' — together 'to make the thought in his heart less' (i.e., to lessen his worry).
segments.s240-g223: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیک is an adjective/adverb 'near' (here adverbial 'near to'). آن مرغزاران is the demonstrative + plural noun 'those meadows'. رسید is the past tense 'he reached/came' — standard subject–verb–complement order (subject implied from previous clause).
segments.s240-g224: ز (poetic form of از) means 'of/from' and introduces the things he expected to find. اسپان و چوپان lists 'horses and shepherds'. نشانی means 'sign/trace/any mark'. ندید is the past negative 'he did not see' (ندید = negated دید). Together: 'he saw no sign of horses or shepherds.'
segments.s240-g209: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (classical usage). The phrase places رستم (proper name) as the subject and شتابندگان را is the definite/direct object marked by را; بدید is a past-tense verb 'saw'. Word order is Subject–Object–Verb (classical Persian), so: 'When Rostam saw the hastening ones.'
segments.s240-g210: This string describes the weapon Rostam draws. سبک (here) is the adjective 'light' (in weight) modifying تیغ; تیغ تیز is a noun + adjective 'sharp sword' (poetic order allows stacked modifiers). از میان is the prepositional combination 'out of/from among' and برکشید is a past transitive verb 'drew up/out' (here: drew a weapon). Together: 'he drew his light, sharp sword out from among (them).'
segments.s240-g211: بغرید is a past verb 'he roared' (intransitive). چون شیر is a simile 'like a lion'. و links the clause to the next action; برگفت here means 'he uttered/said' (classical past), and نام is the direct object 'name' (i.e., he spoke his own name). The sequence is: 'He roared like a lion and spoke his name.'
segments.s240-g212: که introduces reported speech = 'that' / 'namely'. من is the first‑person pronoun 'I'. رستمم is Rostam with the clitic -م indicating 'I am Rostam' (copulative sense in classical Persian). پور is the relational noun 'son/descendant (of)'; دستان here is part of a patronymic (a proper name in context), which is not one of the listed senses; سام is the proper name 'Sam'. Grammatically this clause gives his identification: 'that I am Rostam, son of Dastan, (of) Sam.'
segments.s240-g213: بشمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'with the sword' (بـ + noun). ازیشان = از + ایشان 'from/of them'. دو بهره is a numeric + noun 'two portions/shares' — poetically here meaning 'two (fell) victims' or 'two men'. بکشت is an archaic past verb formed with بـ + کشت (root of 'to kill/strike'), meaning 'he killed/struck'. The clause therefore means 'with the sword he slew two of them.'
segments.s240-g214: چو here is the temporal 'when'. چوپان is the noun 'shepherd' (the person who reacts). چنان 'thus/so' refers back to the prior action; دید is past 'saw'; بنمود is a past verb 'showed/displayed' (here idiomatically 'turned/showed his back'); پشت is 'back'. The clause reads: 'When the shepherd saw that, he turned his back.'
segments.s240-g215: چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چو = 'as/like', باد = 'wind'). از شگفتی literally 'from astonishment' (i.e., being astonished), هم 'also/even', اندر شتاب 'in haste' — these modifiers together describe manner and motive. Grammatically they form a circumstantial phrase modifying the coming: 'like the wind, out of astonishment and in haste...'.
segments.s240-g216: بدیدار here denotes 'to/for the meeting/scene' or 'to the sight'; اسپ is 'steed/horse'; آمد is the simple past 'came'; افراسیاب is the proper name subject. Word order is somewhat inverted (circumstantial phrase first), yielding: 'to the scene a steed came — Afrasiab' i.e. 'Afrasiab came on a horse to the scene.'
segments.s240-g169: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from'. دریا (daryā) is the source 'sea/ocean' and follows immediately. بمردی (bamardī) here is an adverbial/qualifying form meaning 'with manliness' or 'manfully' modifying کشید. به + یکسو (be yeksu) is a direction phrase 'to/into one side' (به = toward, یکسو = one side). کشید is the past tense of کشیدن 'to pull/draw' and is the main verb of the clause; the listed senses did not include 'to pull', so that sense is not in the provided list.
segments.s240-g170: برآمد is a past/perfective verb meaning 'arose/appeared/issued forth' (here 'rose' or 'emerged'). بهامون is an archaic dative/adverbial 'to us' or 'toward us'. و is the coordinating 'and'. خشکی literally 'dryness' here functions as 'dry land' (the object seen). بدید is the past tense of دیدن 'to see' ('saw').
segments.s240-g171: ستایش گرفت is an idiomatic verbal phrase: گرفت (took) with the noun ستایش 'praise' = 'gave/expressed praise' or 'praised'. آفریننده is the noun 'the Creator'; را is the definite object marker showing that آفریننده is the object of the praising.
segments.s240-g172: رهانیده is a past participle/past perfect form 'having freed/delivered'. از introduces the source/ablative 'from' (here 'from evil'). بد modifies تن as 'evil/bad'; تن بنده is 'the servant's body' or 'the body of the servant' (possession/association). The final را marks بنده as the (definite) object of رهانیده. Together the phrase says that (the Creator) had freed the servant (بنده) from the bad/evil of the body.
segments.s240-g173: برآسود is a past-tense simple verb meaning 'he was relieved/at rest'. و coordinates the next clause. بگشاد is here a verbal/past form 'he opened/loosed' (from گشودن). بند میان = 'the bond between' (بند 'fetter/bond', میان 'between' or 'the middle'), so the verb takes that object: 'he loosened the bond between (them)'.
segments.s240-g174: بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and چشمه is the noun 'spring'. بنهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'to place/put' meaning 'he placed/laid/established'. ببر (literally 'tiger') modifies بيان (expression/eloquence) as a poetic epithet: 'the tiger of utterance' = a fierce or bold utterance; Persian allows such noun+noun modifiers in this order. So the clause literally reads 'upon the spring he set (a) tiger (of) speech' i.e. 'he set forth a bold utterance by the spring.'
segments.s240-g175: کمند 'lasso/snare' and سلیحش 'his weapon(s)' form the compound subject/possessive phrase (سلیح + possessive -ش). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بفگند is a poetic verb form from افگندن/فگندن meaning 'to cast/throw' (here used intransitively in a participial/temporal sense 'when ... were cast/fell' or 'when ... became covered'). نم 'moisture/dew' is the object/affected thing — together the clause means 'when his lasso and weapons were wetted by/dew fell (on them)'.
segments.s240-g176: زره را: زره 'armor' with the object marker را (definite object). بپوشید is the past tense 3rd-singular of پوشیدن 'to put on, to don' (here 'he donned/put on'). شیر دژم follows the verb and is the subject 'the grim/sullen lion' (in epic Persian the subject may come after the verb for emphasis). Together: 'the grim lion put on the armor.'
segments.s240-g137: This clause groups a direction + verb and a following relative/conjunction. بدریا به (به + بدریا) is a prepositional phrase 'towards/into the sea' placed before the verb آید. آید is the 3rd-person present form of آمدن ('to come') here functioning in a clause that connects to the following کِه + اندازدم (the relative/causal connector + 1st-person past 'I threw'), creating a compressed relative/temporal idea ('into the sea (to) come — that I threw'). اندازدم is 1st-person past 'I threw/placed' and is linked as the action referred to by the preceding clause; the construction is archaic/poetic and allows inversion of normal spoken order.
segments.s240-g138: This is a nominal phrase with the patient/theme followed by the verb: کفن سینه ماهیان (a shroud — the chest of the fishes) + سازدم (I made). In Persian poetry the possessor/descriptor (سینه ماهیان) precedes the head noun (کفن). سازدم is 1st-person past 'I made' and closes the sentence; syntactically it says the speaker made (or used) the fishes' chests as a shroud.
segments.s240-g139: وگر is the conditional 'if' (literary). گویم is the 1st‑person present form of گفتن ('I say' / 'I command') used here to introduce a quoted command; the supplied sense list did not include the verb sense, so the sense index is unavailable. او را is the marked object 'him' (را marks definiteness/object). بدریا is the directional phrase 'into the sea' and فگن is an archaic verb meaning 'to throw' or 'to cast' — here part of the reported command ('throw him into the sea').
segments.s240-g140: بکوه is the locative/directional 'to the mountain'; افگند is past 'threw/cast' (3rd-person past); بدگهر is an adjective or epithet 'evil‑minded/ill‑fated'; اهرمن is the proper name/title 'Ahriman' (the devil). Word order is Subject (بدگهر اهرمن) after the clause introduced by the conditional, ending with the past verb افگند.
segments.s240-g141: همه 'all' modifies the following noun phrase. واژگونه is an adjective/adverb 'upside‑down/turned over' placed before the verb for emphasis. بود is the past copula 'was' (here past tense), and کار دیو 'the demon's work/affairs' is the subject phrase (possessive noun + possessor). The clause therefore states that all of the demon's affairs were inverted/overturned.
segments.s240-g142: که here introduces a wished/optative clause ('that' / 'so that'); فریادرس is a compound noun 'rescuer' or 'deliverer'; باد is used as an optative particle 'may' (a conventional poetic wish); گیهان is 'the world'; خدیو is a title 'khedive' used here as 'ruler' — the whole clause is a wish: 'may the world's rescuer be the khedive.'
segments.s240-g143: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed literary sequence 'thus gave an answer' (داد here = 'gave', followed by پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the content of that answer. دانای چین is a title phrase 'the wise one of China' (دانای = 'the wise/sage', چین = 'China' used as a place name); word order is adjective/title then place-name, as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s240-g144: یکی داستانی is 'one tale' or 'a certain tale' (یکی = 'a certain/one', داستانی = 'tale'). زدست is an archaic/prepositional phrase 'by the hand of' or 'coming from' (here 'told by' or 'taken from'), and اندرین means 'among these' or 'in these (matters)'. Together they say the sage produced/told one story among these matters.
segments.s240-g257: 'پس' is the narrative connector 'then'. 'پشتشان' is پشت + enclitic pronoun -شان, meaning 'behind them' (locative, attached to the noun). 'رستم گرزدار' places the proper name followed by an epithet 'گرزدار' ('mace-bearing/mace-wielder') — in epic Persian an epithet often follows the name; there is no overt verb in this short phrase, it functions as a temporal/locative introduction to the action that follows.
segments.s240-g258: 'دو فرسنگ' is an adverbial measure phrase 'two parasangs' placed before the verb. 'برسان' here is a past narrative form 'brought/put near' (classical poetic texts commonly omit a final -د of the 3rd-person past: رساند → رسان). Its object is 'ابر بهار' ('the spring-cloud' = ابر + بهار). So the phrase literally says he 'brought the spring-cloud two parasangs (near)'.
segments.s240-g259: 'چو' is the temporal subordinate 'when'. 'برگشت' is the simple past 3rd-person 'returned/turned back'. 'برداشت' is the past 3rd-person 'took up/removed' and governs the coordinated objects 'پیل و رمه' ('elephant and herd'). Word order is typical epic inversion (چو + verb + objects) with the subject (implicit Rostam) understood.
segments.s240-g260: Here 'بنه' functions as a verbal, archaic/polite past form meaning 'he placed/put' (object marking is compacted in poetry). 'هرچ آمد بچنگش' is a relative/nominal clause 'whatever came into his grasp' (هرچ = 'whatever'; آمد = 'came' used in the subordinate clause; بچنگش = بـ + چنگ + -ش 'into his grasp'). 'همه' is an intensive/distributive 'all' attached after the clause: he put all that came into his grasp. (Note: the printed lexical senses for 'بنه' in the word-list are nouns; in the poem this token is a verbal use — no matching noun sense, so use -1 for the listed senses.)
segments.s240-g261: 'بیامد' is the past-intransitive 'came'. In epic verse the verb may appear before a plural subject. 'گرازان' is the plural noun 'boars' or 'a troop of boars' (subject). 'بران' is a deictic preposition/adverb 'to that/thereon' and 'چشمه' is 'spring'; 'باز' is the adverb 'again' placed at the end. Together: 'Boars came again to that spring.'
segments.s240-g262: 'دلش' = دل + enclitic -ش 'his heart'. The compound 'جنگ جویان' is 'war-seekers' = 'fighters/warriors' (جنگ = 'war/battle' + جویان 'seekers'); in Persian compounds the noun and its modifier can appear as two words. 'بچنگ دراز' is the locative/instrumental phrase بـ + چنگ ('into the grasp') with the postpositive adjective 'دراز' ('long') modifying چنگ; the verb that would make the clause fully explicit (e.g., 'افتاد'/'آمد') is omitted, a common epic stylistic device. So the sense is that the war-seekers fell/ended up 'into his long grasp' and his heart was affected (implied).
segments.s240-g263: 'دگر باره' is an adverbial phrase 'once again/again'. 'اکوان' (plural of 'قَوَان'/'اکوان') is a poetic plural 'worlds/heavens' used as subject. 'بدو' = بـ + او 'to him/onto him' (dative), 'باز' 'again' (emphatic adverb), and 'خورد' is the past 3rd-person of خوردن in the idiomatic sense 'befell/struck' (i.e., 'the heavens/worlds fell upon him'). Word order (adverbial + subject + dative + verb) is typical of epic verse.
segments.s240-g264: 'نگشتی' here is an archaic past form from گشتن (sense 'turn/come/arrive'); in context it introduces the reported speech/action and is best read as 'having turned/not turning' (poetic ellipsis is common). 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت = past 'said' — note the supplied sense list gave noun senses for گفت, but here it is the verb 'said'). 'سیر از نبرد' is a short predicative phrase meaning 'sated/satisfied from battle' (سیر = 'satiety/sated', از = 'from', نبرد = 'battle'); together it forms the content of the speech: 'Are you sated with fighting?' (rhetorical).
segments.s240-g145: که introduces a relative/indefinite clause ('who/that'). در آب sets the locative 'in the water'. هر کو is an archaic indefinite 'whoever'. بر آیدش is a compound: بر (prefix/particle) + آیدش ('comes to him') and here means 'his sense returns' (i.e. 'he regains consciousness'); the possessed noun هوش ('awareness') is the affected element.
segments.s240-g146: به مینو is a prepositional goal 'to/into Heaven'. روانش is 'his soul' (possessed with -ش). نبیند is a negated present/subjunctive-form of دیدن ('does not see' / 'will not behold') and Sروش (Soroush) is the agent; word order is verb before agent/object because of the poetic inversion.
segments.s240-g147: بزاری is an archaic/jussive-like form meaning 'be wretched' or 'let him be wretched'; هم adds 'also/emphatically'. ایدر is an older form of 'here'. بماند is a jussive/present ('remain'); بجای literally 'in place' / 'in his place' and connects to the following clause.
segments.s240-g148: خرامش (خرام + ـش) is 'his/its strutting' or 'proud gait'. نیاید is negative present/subjunctive 'will not come / does not come'. بدیگر here means 'to another' (directional), and سرای (poetic older noun) denotes 'house/abode' — together 'will not come to another house'.
segments.s240-g149: به + کوهم = 'into/on my mountain' (کوهم = 'my mountain', -م = my). بینداز is an imperative 'throw (him)!'. تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that / in order that'. ببر و شیر are nouns 'tiger and lion' (not verbs here).
segments.s240-g150: ببینند is the 3rd-person plural jussive/subjunctive ('may they see' / 'they will see') governed by the preceding تا. چنگال مرد دلیر is the object phrase 'the claw(s) of the brave man/hero' (مرد here = valiant man, دلیر = brave). Poetic word order places the verb before its object.
segments.s240-g151: ز رستم is a poetic locative/ablative 'about/from Rostam' (i.e. 'on hearing of Rostam'). چو بشنید = 'when he heard' (چو = 'when'). اکوان دیو is the nominative subject 'Akvan the demon' placed after the verb in the usual epic word order.
segments.s240-g152: برآورد غریو here means 'he produced/raised a cry' (برآورد = 'brought forth/uttered'). بر سوی دریا is a directional phrase 'toward the sea' (بر/سوی marking direction). غریو ('roar/shout') is the thing uttered; word order places the verb and direction before the noun for poetic effect.
segments.s240-g313: This is a noun phrase where the possessor relation is compressed in poetry: درفش شهنشاه = "the standard (banner) of the king/emperor" (ezāfe is omitted). با introduces the accompanying instrument/element and governs کرنای "with trumpets/horns." Word order is Noun + possessor + با + instrument.
segments.s240-g314: ببردند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb (بـ- prefix + بردند) meaning "they carried/took." The following با again marks accompaniment: "with (ژنده) پیل و (درای)." ژنده is an adjective meaning "tattered/ragged" modifying پیل (elephant). و links the last item; درای here is unclear in the supplied senses, so it is treated as an accompanying noun (likely an epithet for attendants or instruments).
segments.s240-g315: چو is the temporal conjunction "when." The sequence رستم درفش جهاندار شاه is a compressed Persian genitive chain (ezāfe omitted): "Rostam" + "the banner of the world‑holder (sovereign) king." In poetic word order the modifying nouns stack without explicit linking particles.
segments.s240-g316: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (a noun meaning 'look, gaze' or 'a pause') + کرد (to do) = "he looked/paused/kept watch." کامد is an older-form verb meaning "he came/appeared." پذیره is a noun "reception," and براه (poetic contraction of بر راه) indicates location/direction "on/toward the road." Together: he paused and came to receive on the road.
segments.s240-g317: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase: فرود (descent/alighting) + آمد (came) = "he alighted/dismounted." The clause خاک را داد بوس uses داد (gave) with the verbal/noun بوس (kiss) in a light-verb idiom: "he gave a kiss to the earth" i.e. "he kissed the ground." را is the object marker marking خاک as definite object.
segments.s240-g318: خروش سپاه آمد is a straightforward S‑Noun + genitive + verb: "the army's cry came/rose." The coordination و بوق و کوس appends instruments/sounds: "and trumpets and drums (sounded)." آمد is the intransitive verb 'came' used here for the arrival of a sound/uproar.
segments.s240-g319: This is an appositive/title cluster in compressed poetic order: سر ... سرکشان = an expression meaning roughly "chief/head of the proud (or chiefs)" (ezāfe omitted), followed by رستم تاج‌بخش = "Rostam, the crown‑bestower" (treat Taj‑bakhsh as an epithet). Poetry often stacks titles without linking particles.
segments.s240-g320: بفرمود is an honorific past tense verb meaning "he commanded/ordered." تا introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause "so that/that," which is followed by the subjunctive verb برنشیند (بر‑ + نشیند) "to sit/ alight upon." برخش in the text is the compressed form of برِ رخش (on Rakhsh, Rostam's horse) — here the object/place of the sitting. The structure: he ordered (something) so that (someone) should mount Rakhsh.

sentences.s240-s158: When Rostam saw the sovereign's standard, he halted and came forward to receive him on the road.
sentences.s240-s159: He dismounted and kissed the earth; the army's cry rose, and horns and drums sounded.
sentences.s240-s160: Rostam, the crown-bestower, commanded that he mount Rakhsh.
